data.title: Drug giant hit by Trump autism claims bought for record $49bn
data.description:
A US drug giant hit by Donald Trump’s claims linking its paracetamol brand to an increased risk of autism has been bought for $49bn (£37bn) after shares crashed to a record low.

On Monday, Kenvue, which makes the Tylenol paracetamol brand, said it had agreed to be acquired by consumer goods giant Kimberly-Clark, which owns Kleenex, Huggies and Andrex.

The takeover comes after Kenvue shares hit their lowest level on record, with $13bn wiped off the company since the start of the year amid heightened scrutiny over Mr Trump’s claims about Tylenol.

In September, the US president said pregnant women should “fight like hell” not to take the paracetamol drug, urging them to “tough it out” when in pain.

Mr Trump went on to claim that the use of Tylenol during pregnancy could make autism more likely, citing unfounded links. The US president said: “Don’t take Tylenol. Don’t take it.”

The claim prompted a backlash from doctors who argue that it puts pregnant women’s health at risk.

The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) issued guidance saying there was “no evidence that taking paracetamol during pregnancy causes autism in children”.

In the UK, Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, urged pregnant women not to pay “any attention whatsoever” to Mr Trump’s claims.

Kenvue said it “strongly disagrees with allegations that it does and is deeply concerned about the health risks and confusion this poses for expecting mothers and parents”.

Last week, Robert F Kennedy Jr, the US health secretary, also admitted that there was not “sufficient” evidence to link Tylenol to autism.

However, Kenvue is still facing a lawsuit in Texas over claims that it deceptively marketed its pain medication to pregnant people despite the alleged autism risk.

Kenvue’s $48.7bn takeover marks the largest buyout of a US consumer goods maker ever recorded, ahead of Mars’s $35.9bn takeover of Kellanova last year.

The offer price is at a 46pc premium to Kenvue’s closing price on Friday.

Kenvue, which was spun out of Johnson & Johnson in 2023, has also been drawn into court cases in the UK over its talc powder.

In October, a lawsuit was filed at the High Court over claims that Johnson & Johnson’s baby powder caused illnesses, including ovarian cancer, between 1965 and 2023.

Kenvue is fighting the claims, saying that it expects a judge to conclude its talc-based baby powder does not cause cancer.

The legal case led to claims that the scrutiny risked putting off potential buyers, with companies including Britain’s Haleon having previously been rumoured as possible buyers.

Kenvue kicked off a process to assess selling either parts or the whole company earlier this year in response to pressure from activist investors.

It ousted its chief executive in July as part of a push to prepare for a sale.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Travel chaos expected for millions on Boxing Day
data.description:
Drivers are being warned to expect a surge in post-Christmas Day traffic as millions of people take to the roads to visit loved ones or go shopping.

The AA estimated 19.7 million car journeys will be made across the UK on Boxing Day.

A survey of more than 10,000 of its members suggested visiting family and friends is the main reason for festive car travel, cited by 60-63 per cent of respondents asked about their plans for the 11 days up to and including Jan 5.

This is closely followed by shopping trips (47-52 per cent), while commuting to work will account for 27-29 per cent of journeys.

Congestion hotspots

It comes as railway closures on Boxing Day are expected to force a record number of travellers onto the roads.

Likely congestion hotspots over the coming days identified by the RAC and Inrix, a transport analytics company, include the M25 clockwise from junctions 7 to 16, the M25 anti-clockwise from junctions 17 to 12 and the M40 northbound from junctions 8A to 10.

Traffic levels are expected to be highest between 10am and 7pm, so drivers are being urged to set off outside that period if possible.

The AA estimated that there will be 18 million car trips on New Year’s Day, which will rise to 20.7 million on Jan 2 and 22 million on Jan 5 as many people return to work and school.

Drivers in the south will be affected by the continued closure of the M27 for major works in both directions between junctions 9 and 11 until 4am on Jan 4.

Shaun Jones, AA expert patrol, said: “Throughout the festive season we see a real mix of activity on the roads.

“Visiting family, picking up shopping and getting back into work all keep people moving locally, even when the long-distance trips are few and far between.

“New Year’s Day stands out as the quietest moment in the whole period, giving drivers a rare chance to avoid congestion.

“But the calm is short-lived. By Jan 2, traffic rebounds fast as work and school return, and breakdown call-outs rise sharply.”

Rail service disruption

Drivers not venturing out over the festive period are advised to get their cars ready for the new year by carrying out checks on their tyres, battery, screenwash and fuel level.

National Highways said it will lift more than 90 per cent of roadworks on its network of motorways and major A roads in England by 6am on Dec 21, and they will not return before the early hours of Jan 2.

Pressure on the roads will be exacerbated by major disruption to rail services continuing for several days.

As usual, the entire network shuts down on Christmas Day and most operators will run no trains on Boxing Day.

National Rail Enquiries warned that most train operators will have no service on Boxing Day this year. However, there will be a very limited train service operated by a small number of operators, including Chiltern Railways, London Overground, Merseyrail, ScotRail, Southern and Stansted Express.

Jerome Mayhew MP, the shadow transport minister, said: “Because Labour do not have the backbone to stand up to their union paymasters, the roads are set to be so busy this Christmas that even this Labour Government won’t be able to make a U-turn.

“Families travelling home this Boxing Day are set to face off against millions of other drivers as Labour have refused to ensure that train operators run on such a busy day. It’s a mess.

“Rather than wasting time and money on new paint jobs for their vanity rail projects, Labour should focus on ensuring our services run when people need them.”

Network Rail engineering projects on multiple sections of the West Coast Main Line will affect journeys to and from London Euston between Saturday and Jan 4, as well as journeys between Scotland and north-west England from New Year’s Day to Jan 14 inclusive.

Elsewhere on the network, no services will run between Stansted Airport and Cambridge North, or between Bury St Edmunds and Cambridge, up to and including Jan 3.

London Waterloo, one of the UK’s busiest stations, is closed to trains until the end of Sunday, and will have a reduced timetable up to the start of Jan 5.

In Scotland, services between Dalmuir and Balloch or Helensburgh Central, and between Glasgow Queen Street and Crianlarich, will only resume on Jan 2.

Some 95 per cent of Britain’s railways will be unaffected by engineering work over the festive period, Network Rail said.

data.title: Brian Palmer, writer of the first TV advertisement shown in Britain, for Gibbs SR toothpaste
data.description:
Brian Palmer, who has died aged 96, wrote and produced the first advertisement on British television and was one of the three founders of KMP, the most innovative advertising agency of the 1960s; he also founded New Solutions, a successful market research company, and was a director of Young’s Brewery.

In later life, he established himself as an artist whose work appealed to a wide audience. His pictures were selected three times for the Royal Academy summer exhibition, and his drawings, etchings and paintings always sold well.

Brian Dudley Buller Palmer was born in Dublin on August 28 1929 to Capt Charles Dudley Palmer, an Anglo-Irish landowner, and Catherine Anne, daughter of Ralph Hughes-Buller. The family moved to Kensington in 1932, just before the birth of their second son, the future General Sir Patrick Palmer. After their parents divorced in 1938 the two boys moved for some years between relatives in England and Scotland.

Boarding school provided an anchor of relative stability for Brian: he attended Wells House and Rugby, and won a scholarship to read English at King’s College, Cambridge. The award covered most, but not all, costs; his father refused to make up the difference, so Palmer did his National Service. He was commissioned and responsible for 20 men in the Royal Artillery before he turned 19.

At school Palmer had been an avid poet and painter, and had won a British Travel Association competition for poster design. When he left the Army he chose commercial art as his pre-release course. He knew he had to make a living but “had no idea what at”.

His father wanted him to become an accountant, and Palmer was nearly compelled to sign away five years to that end. But he was rescued by a chance meeting with a girl on a bus; through her he met the writer Nigel Balchin, who suggested a career in advertising.

Palmer was given a tour of an agency and “fell in love”. He took up a trainee job with CF Higham at £4 per week – “not enough to live on”, even according to his employer. But his work was successful, and he moved to Young & Rubicam as a copywriter in 1952.

Palmer immediately saw the potential of television for advertising. Most of his British contemporaries did not: his boss told him that he was mad to concentrate on TV because “it would never be a major medium”. Palmer persisted, however, and in 1955 he wrote and produced a commercial for Gibbs SR toothpaste, the first television advertisement broadcast in Britain – at 8.13pm on September 22 1955.

Broadcast during a break in a variety show hosted by Jack Jackson, the ad featured a tube of toothpaste embedded in a block of ice. Viewers also saw the “plug” girl Meg Smith brushing her teeth “up and down and round the gums”, while Alex Macintosh announced in faultless BBC diction: “The tingling fresh toothpaste that does your gums good, too. It’s tingling fresh. It’s fresh as ice. It’s Gibbs SR toothpaste.”

At 30, Palmer was Young & Rubicam’s youngest ever director, but he now wanted a different challenge. In 1964, with David Kingsley and Michael Manton, he founded KMP on radical new principles. It was an instant success, rapidly acquiring major accounts such as Regent (Texaco) and Cunard.

In 1965, KMP became the first British advertising agency to open an office in New York. In 1966, Palmer launched a campaign for White Horse whisky, with the line “You can take a White Horse anywhere”. It was revolutionary and ran for years. In 1969, KMP became the first British advertising agency to go public.

From 1973 Palmer stepped back from KMP and joined Young’s, as director of the new wines and spirits department. He was headhunted in 1979 to run Doyle Dane Bernbach, a leading American advertising agency. He rapidly returned the company to profitability, then in 1983 left to found the strategic marketing consultancy, New Solutions.

Throughout his advertising career, Palmer had continued to draw and paint. Then in 1991 the instructor at his evening class told him he should be a professional artist. From then on, he devoted himself to art.

In 1998, aged 69, he earned an honours degree in Fine Art. His paintings, drawings and etchings, many of which included poems, were regularly exhibited and purchased. He was working on new ones when he died.

Palmer had a seemingly inexhaustible fountain of optimism and good cheer, allied to a rare ability to identify the best in those he met, and to see ways out of difficult, even terrible, situations in which he or others found themselves.

Palmer married Brenda Attkins, whom he met at CF Higham, in 1953. She died in 2003, and he is survived by a daughter and son. Another daughter died in 2018.

Brian Palmer, born August 28 1929, died December 7 2025

data.title: NHS to pay £3bn more for drugs to escape Trump tariffs
data.description:
The NHS has agreed to pay 25pc more for new medicines to escape Donald Trump’s sweeping new tariffs on drugs.

The US president unveiled a new pharmaceutical trade deal with Britain on Monday, striking an agreement to impose zero import tariffs on medical products from the UK.

It will see British medicines carved out from sweeping new levies, offering a major boost for one of Britain’s biggest exports to America. In 2024, the UK exported around £6.6bn in pharmaceutical and medical products to the US.

The agreement includes a pledge for the NHS to increase the net price it pays for new medicines by 25pc, which will add around £3bn to the taxpayer’s £12bn annual medical spending bill. This will be phased in.

It follows weeks of uncertainty over the future of the industry. In August, the US president threatened to impose tariffs of up to 250pc on all pharmaceutical imports – including the UK.

In September, he said there would be 100pc tariffs on “any branded or patented Pharmaceutical Product” coming into the US from October 1. At the time, he said companies could get an exemption if they had started building factories in the US.

While the White House has agreed to a spate of country-specific trade deals over the past year, pharma tariffs are expected to fall outside the scope of these agreements.

Under the new agreement, the NHS has also committed to slashing rebates that drug companies have to pay on sales of newer medicines to the health service.

The so-called Voluntary Scheme for Branded Medicines Pricing, Access and Growth (VPAG) is designed to cap the overall NHS drug bill but pharmaceutical companies have complained it has failed to keep pace with inflation.

Additionally, the deal means the UK will cut the rebate rate from 22.9pc to 15pc in 2026.

It is also understood that the Government will increase the threshold that determines which treatments are available on the NHS, boosting the spending cap on certain medicines and paving the way for more cutting-edge treatments to be introduced.

Drug giants have long complained over the decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money, claiming this does not allow them to get the best new drugs into the NHS.

Jamieson Greer, the US trade representative, said: “For too long, American patients have been forced to subsidise prescription drugs and biologics in other developed countries by paying a significant premium for the same products in ours.

“Today, the United States and the United Kingdom announce this negotiated outcome pricing for innovative pharmaceuticals, which will help drive investment and innovation in both countries.”

He added: “The Trump Administration is reviewing the pharmaceutical pricing practices of many other US trading partners and hopes that they will follow suit with constructive negotiations.”

Howard Lutnick, the US commerce secretary, said the deal would bring new jobs to America.

Mr Lutnick said: “This deal doesn’t just deepen our economic partnership with the United Kingdom – it ensures that the breakthroughs of tomorrow will be built, tested and produced on American soil.”

The White House has previously made clear its frustration over the lack of spending on innovative medicines in Britain. Currently, the UK spends just 0.28pc of its GDP on new medicines, roughly a third of what America does.

Britain previously pledged to review the health service’s drug pricing to take into account “the concerns of the president” under the UK-US trade deal struck in May.

Mr Trump has called for an end to overseas countries “freeloading” on American innovation, singling out Britain for a place where medicine prices are unfairly low compared to the US.

The US ambassador last month said Britain needed to change how it priced medicines or risk more global drug companies scrapping investments in the UK.

Merck, one of America’s largest drug companies, this year ditched plans to open a £1bn research centre in London, while AstraZeneca has paused £200m spending on its Cambridge research facility.

Liz Kendall, the Science and Technology Secretary, said the deal would “enable and incentivise life sciences companies to continue to invest and innovate right here in the UK”.

Chris Boerner, chief executive of Bristol Myers Squibb, said the trade agreement meant the pharmaceutical company will invest “upwards of $500m (£378m)” over the next five years.

Mr Boerner said: “This investment will come across multiple areas of the business including research, development and manufacturing. This agreement is a sign of progress and one that creates an environment conducive to our continued presence in the UK.”

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: Banks in mortgage war as rates plunge
data.description:
Mortgage lenders have launched a pre-Christmas “rates war” ahead of an expected cut to the Bank Rate next week.

Both Natwest and Barclays cut their rates on Monday, with headline rates for the former dropping to as little as 3.62pc for a two-year fixed deal, or a 3.66pc “product transfer” for those borrowers looking to remortgage with the lender.

Barclays dropped rates on its two-year mortgages by as much as 0.10 percentage points to 3.70pc. The lender cut rates for its five-year fixes before last month’s Budget.

More than 20 banks cut their rates last week, including some of the largest lenders such as Santander and Nationwide.

Santander has launched rates of 3.51pc for those moving home – but the offer was only available on loans between £500,000 and £2m. Nationwide recently cut the interest on its deals to the lowest rate since 2022, with a 3.58pc two-year fix.

Rachel Springall, of analysts Moneyfacts, said: “Mortgage rates continue on the downward trend and November was particularly fruitful for fixed rate cuts.

“The re-pricing by lenders led to the average five-year fixed rate dropping below 5pc for the first time in over two years and sits at its lowest point since before the ‘mini-Budget’ in September 2022, alongside its two-year counterpart.”

Hina Bhudia, a partner at Knight Frank Finance, said: “The Budget removed some uncertainty over the path of borrowing costs, which is giving lenders the scope to keep trimming mortgage rates.

“This bodes well for 2026. Purchasing activity is running in line with 2019 levels despite this period of fiscal uncertainty, suggesting there is pent-up demand among purchasers. We should see that released as mortgage rates drift lower and we move into the new year.”

Jack Tutton, of brokers SJ Mortgages, said: “The market is hotting up due to the strong prediction of a Bank Rate cut before Christmas and potentially more cuts to come in 2026.

“Mortgage holders will be hoping that these cuts are a gift that keeps on giving and more reductions from more lenders will follow.”

Mortgage rates surged in the wake of Liz Truss’s disastrous “mini-Budget” in 2022, having been at record low levels during the pandemic. By August 2023, the average five-year fix had hit 6.85pc, while the average two-year deal peaked at 6.22pc.

But the average two-year fixed mortgage rate on Monday was 4.85pc, according to Moneyfacts. The average five-year deal was slightly higher, at 4.91pc. The average two-year tracker rate was 4.66pc.

The Bank Rate is widely expected to fall from its current rate of 4pc to 3.75pc at a meeting of the Monetary Policy Committee next Thursday. There have been three cuts so far this year, with more priced in for next year.

Inflation fell to 3.6pc in October, from 3.8pc, which gave the central bank more room to cut the official interest rate, and meant mortgage lenders began to cut their rates.

data.title: Starmer to push Britain into stricter net zero targets under EU deal
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer is preparing to tie Britain to the EU’s net zero plans in a move that would impose radically stricter clean energy targets on homes and businesses.

The Prime Minister and Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, are negotiating for Britain to rejoin the EU’s internal electricity market, which treats the 27 countries of the EU and Norway as a single borderless power grid.

The EU will only let Britain back into the system if Sir Keir agrees to the bloc’s ambitious targets for renewable energy, which would require the UK to decarbonise not just electricity but also heating and transport rapidly.

In practice, it would mean net zero targets would need to be doubled.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, accused the Prime Minister of “surrendering control of our energy system to bureaucrats in Brussels”.

She said: “UK ministers will be forced to reduce emissions regardless of what it will do to people’s energy bills or the competitiveness of our businesses.”

It comes as Labour seeks to forge closer ties with the EU, with MPs debating in recent weeks whether Britain should return to the customs union.

The plans would also aid Mr Miliband’s ambitions to decarbonise the power grid, allowing the UK to import foreign electricity when low wind or sunshine cuts output from wind and solar farms.

The EU’s demands emerged in a document placed without fanfare on the Cabinet Office website. The plan is both technically challenging and politically sensitive because it would make UK energy policy subject to EU jurisdiction.

It stated: “The Electricity Agreement should ... set an indicative global target for the share of renewable energy in the gross final consumption of energy in the United Kingdom. To ensure a level playing field, the global target should be comparable to that of the European Union.”

The EU’s target is that 42.5pc of all its energy should come from renewables by 2030, with an aspiration to reach 45pc.

This is roughly double the UK’s current level of 22pc.

Mr Miliband has set a target to decarbonise Britain’s power generation by 95pc by 2030, but electricity accounts for only 20pc of UK total energy consumption, so this will never be enough to meet EU demands. Transport, heating and industrial energy account for 75pc of the UK’s total energy consumption.

At the moment, the UK gets about 75pc of its total energy from oil and gas, a level that has hardly changed in decades.

Energy experts said the EU target could only be achieved by accelerating the replacement of boilers with heat pumps, adding more biofuels to petrol and diesel, and encouraging even faster adoption of electric vehicles.

‘Billpayers should be very worried’

David Turver, an energy analyst, cast doubt over the feasibility of the target even with more ambitious action.

He said: “Whether it’s a 42.5pc or 45pc target by 2030, it doesn’t matter. It’s totally unachievable. Unless, of course, they totally crush overall energy consumption and the whole of Europe deindustrialises and goes back to 19th century levels of energy use.”

Ms Coutinho said: “Billpayers should be very worried about what the Labour Government is signing up to. The EU’s Renewable Energy Directive is like all the worst parts of the UK Climate Change Act – on steroids.”

Britain left the EU internal electricity market in 2021 as part of Brexit – but has since become increasingly dependent on its European neighbours to keep its lights on.

Power generated in France, Holland, Belgium, Norway and Denmark reaches the UK via seven interconnectors – subsea cables – with plans for several more.

On Tuesday, about 18pc of the UK’s electricity was being generated overseas, mostly in France, Norway and Denmark.

On some days recently more than half the electricity consumed in London and the south east is generated in France.

The UK’s exclusion from the EU market means its traders are banned from using the automated algorithms that optimise cross-border flows and have to buy and sell manually.

They also have to buy interconnector capacity and electricity in separate transactions. The “clunkiness” of such trading adds costs estimated at up to £370m a year.

Barnaby Wharton, the head of grid policy at Renewable UK, which represents the wind industry, said greater alignment with Europe would lower costs by improving the effeciency of the system.

A Cabinet Office spokesman said that agreed text exchanged between the EU and UK would form the basis for further talks in 2026.

They said: “Closer cooperation on electricity will bring real benefits to British businesses and consumers – helping to drive down energy costs, strengthen energy security and drive investment in the North Sea. We will not give a running commentary on ongoing talks.”

data.title: Nationwide hit with record fine after failing to spot customer’s £27m Covid fraud
data.description:
Nationwide has been hit with a record fine after failing to spot a customer’s £27.3m furlough fraud.

The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) has ordered the British lender to pay £44m as a result of its failings, which included the incident during Covid.

The City watchdog revealed in a ruling on Friday that a Nationwide customer deposited £26m into their current account over the space of eight days, having received 24 furlough payments totalling £27.3m over 13 months.

HMRC was able to recover £26.5m from the fraud, but roughly £800,000 remains unaccounted for.

The FCA added that between October 2016 and July 2021, Nationwide had ineffective systems for keeping up to date with due diligence and risk assessments for all its personal current account customers.

The fine was announced just a day after Dame Debbie Crosbie, the chief executive of Nationwide, was appointed as the Government’s new Women in Finance champion.

As part of its findings, the FCA said the bank was aware of its customers using personal accounts for business transactions, which was a breach of its terms because it did not yet offer business accounts.

This left Nationwide unable to effectively monitor money-laundering risks, the regulator said.

Therese Chambers, of the FCA, said: “Nationwide failed to get a proper grip of the financial crime risks lurking within its customer base.

“It took too long to address its flawed systems and weak controls, meaning red flags were missed with serious consequences.

“Building societies and banks have a key role in the fight against financial crime. Firms must remain vigilant in this fight.”

A Nationwide spokesman said it had identified the issues through its own review and voluntarily brought them to the attention of the FCA.

The bank would have been fined almost £63m, but qualified for a 30pc discount after fully cooperating with the FCA and agreeing to resolve the issues. The £44m fine is the biggest Nationwide has ever received.

Nationwide said it has since invested significantly in its economic crime control framework, adding that it did not believe that the issues caused financial loss to any of its customers.

“We are sorry that our controls during the period fell below the high standards we expect,” the spokesman added.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Thousands of North Sea jobs saved after Petrofac rescue deal
data.description:
Around 3,000 UK jobs have been saved after a US rival bought Petrofac’s North Sea operations.

In a rescue deal announced on Christmas Eve, Texas-based Chicago Bridge & Iron Company (CB&I) has taken over Petrofac’s Aberdeen-based engineering and operations arm.

It comes after the business was plunged into uncertainty this year when the Petrofac holding company entered administration.

This stemmed from financial challenges across its global operations, as its UK arm was deemed profitable.

Petrofac’s North Sea division manages platforms for oil and gas giants such as BP, Shell, Ithaca and EnQuest. It is also responsible for building and maintaining a series of UK wind farms.

The threat of collapse had alarmed Government officials owing to its importance to both the North Sea oil and gas industry and Ed Miliband’s net zero ambitions.

Announcing the deal on Wednesday, Tareq Kawash, the chief executive of Petrofac, said: “This is a great outcome for the Petrofac Asset Solutions business, supporting job security for 3,000 talented team members.”

CB&I is a leading global designer and builder of storage facilities, tanks, and terminals for the energy industry.

Following the agreement, Mark Butts, the company’s chief executive, said: “Our organisations share similar management philosophies and industry-leading safety performance.

“We see clear opportunities to enhance performance and deliver stable cash flow generation. These factors collectively support CB&I’s long-term growth objectives.”

Exact details of the deal are not yet known. Still, it is understood that CB&I will not assume any of Petrofac’s debts, which were previously estimated at $707.9m (£560m).

Petrofac’s recent challenges

Petrofac was founded in Texas 44 years ago and later expanded to the UK, establishing an engineering centre in Woking and an operational base in Aberdeen.

It floated on the London Stock Exchange in 2005, offering training, engineering services, and the operation of entire platforms for a series of North Sea producers.

However, it has been challenged by several issues in recent years.

The company’s Gulf division was caught up in a corruption scandal in the 2010s, resulting in a £77m fine following an investigation by the Serious Fraud Office.

It was then hit by the pandemic, which caused construction delays, disrupted cash flow and forced Petrofac to halt debt repayments.

Its problems intensified earlier this year after a long-running restructuring deal collapsed after a major customer cancelled a lucrative contract.

Petrofac Limited, the holding company, appointed administrators soon after.

James Bennett, of Teneo, Petrofac’s administrator, said: “This is a very positive outcome and secures the future of its operations and the roles of many highly skilled people.”

data.title: Thousands of North Sea jobs saved after Petrofac rescue deal
data.description:
Around 3,000 UK jobs have been saved after a US rival bought Petrofac’s North Sea operations.

In a rescue deal announced on Christmas Eve, Texas-based Chicago Bridge & Iron Company (CB&I) has taken over Petrofac’s Aberdeen-based engineering and operations arm.

It comes after the business was plunged into uncertainty this year when the Petrofac holding company entered administration.

This stemmed from financial challenges across its global operations, as its UK arm was deemed profitable.

Petrofac’s North Sea division manages platforms for oil and gas giants such as BP, Shell, Ithaca and EnQuest. It is also responsible for building and maintaining a series of UK wind farms.

The threat of collapse had alarmed Government officials owing to its importance to both the North Sea oil and gas industry and Ed Miliband’s net zero ambitions.

Announcing the deal on Wednesday, Tareq Kawash, the chief executive of Petrofac, said: “This is a great outcome for the Petrofac Asset Solutions business, supporting job security for 3,000 talented team members.”

CB&I is a leading global designer and builder of storage facilities, tanks, and terminals for the energy industry.

Following the agreement, Mark Butts, the company’s chief executive, said: “Our organisations share similar management philosophies and industry-leading safety performance.

“We see clear opportunities to enhance performance and deliver stable cash flow generation. These factors collectively support CB&I’s long-term growth objectives.”

Exact details of the deal are not yet known. Still, it is understood that CB&I will not assume any of Petrofac’s debts, which were previously estimated at $707.9m (£560m).

Petrofac’s recent challenges

Petrofac was founded in Texas 44 years ago and later expanded to the UK, establishing an engineering centre in Woking and an operational base in Aberdeen.

It floated on the London Stock Exchange in 2005, offering training, engineering services, and the operation of entire platforms for a series of North Sea producers.

However, it has been challenged by several issues in recent years.

The company’s Gulf division was caught up in a corruption scandal in the 2010s, resulting in a £77m fine following an investigation by the Serious Fraud Office.

It was then hit by the pandemic, which caused construction delays, disrupted cash flow and forced Petrofac to halt debt repayments.

Its problems intensified earlier this year after a long-running restructuring deal collapsed after a major customer cancelled a lucrative contract.

Petrofac Limited, the holding company, appointed administrators soon after.

James Bennett, of Teneo, Petrofac’s administrator, said: “This is a very positive outcome and secures the future of its operations and the roles of many highly skilled people.”

data.title: Britain must act now or risk losing our sovereign defence capabilities
data.description:
Britain’s defence industry is the envy of the world but this key sector now stands at a perilous crossroads.

With military spending set to rise and the Ministry of Defence (MoD) preparing its long-awaited defence investment plan, there is a genuine opportunity to convert increased budgets into more industrial production.

But that will only happen if Britain backs its own industry now.

For decades, the UK has sustained world-leading expertise across the aerospace, naval and advanced manufacturing sectors, earning its status as a net defence exporter. Yet that reputation and the sovereign capabilities underpinning it are at risk.

Sovereign manufacturing strength not only underpins our core national security but is the foundation of Britain’s role as a global defence power.

These capabilities – from composite materials for next-generation platforms to aircraft canopies and advanced propulsion systems – have been built through years of investment and innovation. But they are more fragile than they appear.

Warfare is evolving rapidly. Drones are now central to modern defence.

The UK must back British industry in developing these capabilities – which means supporting companies and their supply chains.

Without government backing, skills and production will shift abroad to competitors in the US and Europe. Once lost, such capability is hard to rebuild.

Allies and competitors alike understand the stakes. The US prefers to work with long-term partners such as the UK – but if British industrial capacity proves unreliable, Washington can easily turn to Canada, Australia or European producers such as France and Germany, where defence spending is also accelerating.

The solution is straightforward. Defence procurement must prioritise building and sustaining UK industrial capacity.

The MoD has announced a new competition for drones in early 2026. This is an immediate opportunity to demonstrate commitment to UK companies and their supply chains, developing the capabilities the country needs.

The Government has made welcome progress through a number of initiatives but these efforts need coherent delivery across skills, regional development and research funding with defence sovereignty as the guiding objective.

There is also significant untapped potential in better exploiting the UK’s expertise. Our civil aerospace sector is world-class, yet defence procurement seldom leverages its strengths.

Companies that operate across both civil and military domains can bring commercial efficiency and scale to defence programmes. This civil-to-defence technology transfer is exactly the kind of competitive advantage the UK should be exploiting.

The industrial base is ready to invest and respond.

At Melrose, we are investing over £20m in advanced composite manufacturing at our Isle of Wight site specifically for future air vehicles. We are also training 100 apprentices at Cowes over the next 12 months.

At our global technology centre in Bristol, where we employ 300 skilled people, we are developing advanced technologies that transfer directly into defence applications.

We are not alone – look to BAE Systems, Babcock and Rolls-Royce for similar examples across the sector.

Melrose and the UK’s world-class aerospace and defence industry stand ready to deliver sovereign defence capability and – in parallel – economic growth and skilled jobs.

Our message to government is clear: use British industry or lose it.

The technologies, skills and supply chains exist today but they will not wait indefinitely.

With sustained commitment and strategic coordination, the UK can translate higher defence spending into industrial leadership and lasting competitive advantage.

The decisions made in the coming months will determine whether we seize that opportunity or let it slip away.

Peter Dilnot is chief executive of Melrose Industries, a FTSE 100 aerospace and defence manufacturer. He previously served as a British Army helicopter pilot including with Nato and the UN

data.title: Earl’s Court redevelopment approved despite backlash over ‘bland’ plans
data.description:
The £10bn plan to redevelop Earl’s Court has been approved a decade after the venue was demolished, despite backlash from residents over the “bland” project.

The 4,000-home project is set to proceed after councillors at the Royal Borough of Kensington and Chelsea (RBKC) gave it the go-ahead on Tuesday.

Plans include 2.5 million sq ft of workspace, three cultural venues and 20 acres of public realm, with 35pc of its homes made affordable.

It is one of the biggest development projects approved in London over the past five years.

Developers had been awaiting the final decision after the project cleared the first planning hurdle in November, when neighbouring Hammersmith and Fulham council approved the project.

Approval has come despite local resistance. The development garnered 581 objections, according to a planning report filed with RBKC, versus 182 letters of support.

Those opposing the scheme in recent months criticised the project as “bland” in letters to the council, with one resident calling it an “uncreative Lego build with miserable-looking buildings”.

A second resident said its tall housing towers, with one as high as 42 storeys, would create a “depressing canyon”.

Concerns had also been raised that Earl’s Court would become a playground for rich investors, with its new high-rise buildings left empty by absent landlords.

“This application represents ‘just another high-rise luxury scheme’ that prioritises density and commercial gain over community benefit,” said another resident.

During RBKC’s committee vote on Tuesday, councillors praised the scheme, with the Conservative councillor James Husband calling the buildings “rather attractive”.

Consent comes a decade after the Earls Court Exhibition Centre was demolished, which left behind a 44-acre stretch of land that became central London’s largest empty site.

Up until now, developers have struggled to begin construction amid protracted debate over how the area should look and its level of affordable housing.

The project is led by the Earls Court Development Company (ECDC), a joint venture between the property developer Delancey, the Dutch pension fund APG and the property arm of Transport for London.

After buying the site in 2019, ECDC spent four years devising its proposal before filing it with both councils in September last year.

The first stage will deliver about 1,300 homes across six buildings, including the 42-storey skyscraper. It will also feature a zero-carbon energy network. 

Construction of the overall project is expected to be fully completed in 2041.

Rob Heasman, chief executive of ECDC, said: “Our plans will restore Earl’s Court as a global destination, a place for ingenuity, for the extraordinary and for people to wonder.

“Projects of this scale require ambition, partnership and patience to bring forward, and our ambition has been shaped by four years of engagement, listening and working in collaboration with our community.”

Jamie Ritblat, founder and executive chairman of Delancey, said: “This is a hugely important milestone. Earl’s Court will be a beacon for the capital’s future as a leading global city.

“It will create 23,500 jobs across the country, a new centre for the UK’s climate innovation sector, circa 4,000 new homes and benefit the UK economy by £3bn per year.”

data.title: Channel Tunnel owner blames tax rises as it scraps UK investment
data.description:
The owner of the Channel Tunnel has scrapped all UK investment following the Government’s decision to burden it with tens of millions of pounds in extra taxes.

Eurotunnel said plans to increase its business rates bill by almost 200pc were “unjustified and confiscatory in nature”.

As a result, it said it was suspending plans to develop a freight terminal in Barking, east London, which would have allowed it to run dedicated freight services from France and free up capacity on passenger routes.

The Valuation Office Agency (VOA), which provides the valuations across England and Wales that underpin property taxes, has informed the company that it plans to almost triple the rateable value of its properties from next year.

This would increase Eurotunnel’s annual business rates bill from £22m to £65m in the coming years, although the increase would be phased in, meaning its bill in 2026 will initially jump to £35m.

The VOA has been tasked with overhauling business rates by Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, as she wants companies with bigger premises to pay more in levies to help reduce costs for smaller sites.

“Eurotunnel expresses its deep disagreement with this planned increase and considers it is unjustified and confiscatory in nature, and that it threatens Eurotunnel’s investment capacity in Great Britain,” the company said on Thursday.

“Eurotunnel will continue its efforts to negotiate with the VOA and its supervisory authorities until the final valuation decision, expected at the end of March 2026.”

Yann Leriche, the chief executive, told the Financial Times that the move “makes all of our investments loss-making, so we won’t be making any more investments”.

He added: “As of today, we have frozen our investment in rail assets in the UK.”

He said the suspended investment amounted to £15m, while Eurotunnel suggested it could challenge the decision.

Cancelling plans to boost freight through the Channel Tunnel would conflict with the Government’s hopes of taking cargo off the roads by boosting rail.

“Eurotunnel believes that it is currently unduly penalised compared to its competitors whose activities are more carbon-intensive, less-taxed, and some of which are deviating from the social models applicable in France and Great Britain,” the company said.

Earlier this week, the boss of airline Jet2 complained that the Government was treating the travel industry like a “cash cow”.

This month, electric vehicle charging companies wrote to the Treasury warning that plans to introduce business rates on chargers’ parking bays would hurt the adoption of EVs.

A VOA spokesman said: “The VOA is responsible for non-domestic valuations which reflect changes in the property market. All valuations are carried out by experienced professionals in accordance with industry best practice and legal requirements. The VOA does not determine business rates and next year’s liability has not yet been confirmed.

“We have engaged with Getlink and their advisers on multiple occasions over the past 18 months to discuss their valuation and fully explain our approach. These discussions remain ongoing and we are committed to continuing constructive engagement.

“Ratepayers have the right to formally challenge their valuation with the VOA if they believe it is inaccurate. Should they remain dissatisfied with our response, they may appeal to the independent Valuation Tribunal.”

data.title: Rail boss to exit after ghost train humiliation
data.description:
The chief executive of Britain’s rail regulator has said he will stand down, just days after an embarrassing climbdown over plans to run a high-speed service from Manchester to London without any passengers.

John Larkinson will leave the Office of Rail and Road (ORR) in April after more than 20 years at the regulator, the last seven of them as chief executive.

The announcement comes after Mr Larkinson was forced to concede defeat in a row over the 7am Avanti service from Manchester to London, which the ORR had insisted should run empty.

The regulator argued that the move was necessary to guarantee the train’s availability in the capital for morning services to the North.

Ministers intervened to compel Mr Larkinson to overturn the decision and open the train up to passengers following a public outcry.

While the ORR said Mr Larkinson had informed Declan Collier, the ORR chairman, of his plans to retire a month ago, the timing of the announcement risks stoking speculation about a widening rift between the regulator and the Department for Transport (DfT).

Days before the controversy over the Manchester service, Mr Larkinson and Stephanie Tobyn, the ORR’s strategy director, laid out their concerns about the Government’s rail nationalisation plan.

They warned the House of Commons Transport Committee late last month that the ORR would no longer be the ultimate decision-maker over who should have access to scarce operating routes on the busiest railway lines.

Instead, such rulings will be taken by Great British Railways (GBR) and the DfT.

It is understood that senior staff were informed only yesterday of Mr Larkinson’s exit. He first joined the ORR’s predecessor, the Strategic Rail Authority, in 2000.

The ORR said it would “now start the process of recruiting a successor”.

Reason for stepping down

Mr Larkinson said in an ORR statement that there was “never a perfect time to move on” while indicating that his decision had been influenced by the imminent arrival of GBR.

He said: “Most of my tenure as chief executive has been against a backdrop of rail reform and I feel that the introduction of the Railways Bill into Parliament is an appropriate time to depart.”

He added that “as an independent and expert body with a strong commitment to transparency, the ORR has played a crucial role in the development of the rail and road sectors” and that it should “continue to do so into the future”.

The ORR reinstated the Manchester service under pressure from Peter Hendy, the Rail Minister, and Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, who said “passengers were rightly baffled” by the so-called ghost train.

She added that nationalisation “will ensure problems like these are eliminated”.

Despite its climbdown, the regulator has continued to insist that the service posed a risk to smooth operations on Europe’s busiest intercity route.

A spokesman said: “While we believe the running of this 7am service could have a negative impact on reliability and punctuality of services on the west coast mainline, we understand the importance of this service and we’ll support its continued operation.”

data.title: ‘I’m being forced to sell 200 acres of my land for a railway line’
data.description:
Edward Banks’s family has worked the land in the bucolic village of Harlton, outside Cambridge, since the 1800s.

But his land stands in the path of the new East West Railway (EWR Co), a huge project to restore the historic “Varsity” rail link between Cambridge and Oxford.

He now faces the prospect of being forced to sell up, losing more than 200 acres of his land to a compulsory purchase order (CPO).

After three years of trying to get answers from EWR Co, he has received no information about what the company may offer him.

“It is a shadow over the map,” says Banks, 44, who runs a business hosting weddings in a 400-year-old Grade II-listed barn with his wife Paula, 40. “You have to imagine a worst-case scenario. For me, it’s just really sad. I’m in this limbo.”

As Britain gets busier, and more huge infrastructure projects pop up across the country – from railways and roads to regeneration – more landowners are encountering the unfairness of compulsory purchase.

“Without these CPO powers, the developers would have to successfully negotiate all the land and property rights they need to deliver the projects,” says Fergus Charlton, a partner in law firm Michelmores’ planning and environment team. “One recalcitrant landowner could snowball the whole scheme.”

‘You’re totally powerless’

Banks lives next door to the barn with his wife, son Bertie, 15, and daughter Hebe, 12, in the five-bedroom 18th-century Manor Farm.

As far as he’s aware, his family home will be about 600 metres away from the railway line: “We won’t be able to see it due to existing vegetation, but we will be able to hear it.”

He’s finding the lack of answers extremely stressful: “You’re totally powerless. You do get a lot of generic information, booklets and so on, but you can’t contact them [EWR Co], and there’s no one to speak to. I had one meeting in September 2024. I thought it was quite productive. But I haven’t heard back from them. Anything I said or suggested was probably left in the back of their car.”

After five generations of farming – Banks completed his first harvest when he was 13 – the family started tenanting their land in 2019, around 1,300 acres, to agribusiness P.X. Farms Ltd. Banks has now diversified into weddings and holiday accommodation and owns a GPS supply company.

EWR Co has so far told Banks that it intends to take the 175 acres of arable land he owns, plus 32 acres of grassland he had hoped to use to establish an equine business. This would all be for the railway itself and works storage, as well as to create wildlife biodiversity net gain and flooding mitigation measures.

“That would be crippling to my enterprise here, the equine grazing side of things,” Banks says. “It means I simply won’t have enough grass. They’ve just looked at a map. They’ve sat there in an office far, far away; they haven’t looked at the site, at the ground, how it lies, where the vegetation exists now or the trees. They’ve just gone: ‘We’ll just take that whole field, bosh.’”

The difficulty for Banks and his fellow landowners and residents is the uncertainty. “It was only in May this year that we got on to more detailed information from EWR Co,” Banks says. “Up until then, all we knew was that [the railway was] coming this way; they would like to negotiate with us, but they’ll just do a CPO otherwise. The general public doesn’t understand the process; you don’t know what you should be doing.”

They will, however, potentially benefit from the addition of “hope value” on any land that is taken up by a CPO. This means if it is likely that the land will obtain consent for alternative use, such as housing, then the amount paid takes into account its higher potential value.

An EWR Co spokesman said: “We recognise that the uncertainty for land and property owners as we develop the scheme is difficult. Our ambition is to offer clarity as soon as we can, whilst also listening to the feedback from communities as we evolve the design. Until we have the final designs completed, our intent is to keep those impacted informed. Our conversations with Mr Banks continue as we discuss the support available, including statutory compensation.

“We’re sorry to hear Mr Banks is dissatisfied with the communication he has received. We will contact them again to ensure they have the information they need and that their questions continue to be answered.”

‘You don’t know if it’s going to be pulled away’

George Hurrell, 34, farms livestock and crops across 5,000 acres at Newton Farms in the villages of Newton and Swaffham Prior near Cambridge. He is also facing compulsory purchase.

“I’m not anti-infrastructure projects; it just has to be done in the right way,” says Hurrell, who is married to Millie, 33, and has two young children.

He says one of the most infuriating aspects is that EWR Co keeps changing the boundaries. “When they originally came to see us, they wanted about 100 acres of our land and about 300 acres of my uncle’s, whom we also farm for.

“It’s quite difficult to invest in the land when you don’t know if it’s going to be compulsorily purchased. There’s not much incentive to put farmyard manure on, or farm it in a regenerative way, if you don’t know when it’s all going to be pulled away.”

An EWR spokesman said: “Since the proposals are not final, the project’s anticipated boundary (known as the Draft Order Limits) is still changing. We understand this can cause uncertainty, and we continue to work with those impacted to share their feedback. Our ambition is to understand individual circumstances and, where possible, reduce impacts on their land and property as we continue to develop the designs for the railway.”

Hurrell concedes that he’s “getting away relatively lightly”. He adds: “Our neighbour is facing losing his whole farm. There are lots of people a lot worse off.”

There’s also a catch to the money that comes with a CPO. Should he sell via CPO, Hurrell must roll that cash into an agricultural purpose within 18 months, or he will trigger a capital gains tax bill (the amount of which depends on the land’s ownership structure).

It’s unfair, he says, that because of the railway coming, he has “got to go and buy a random bit of land somewhere in the middle of nowhere and then go and farm it”. Finding 100 acres locally, unaffected by the railway, is a significant challenge.

In South Cambridgeshire, general agricultural values are around £10,000 to £12,500 per acre. “But there is a limited supply,” says Jonathan Purkiss, director of rural services at estate agency Cheffins.

The railway line is planned to run 45m from the cottage of Hurrell’s tied agricultural tenant, an elderly retired farmworker, who faces homelessness because his cottage will become unliveable.

“The tenant, he’s heard nothing. East West Rail says they’re not blighting the property, so they won’t consider it as part of the CPO. However, it will be unsellable once the works take place,” says Hurrell.

“The tenant becomes the [local] council’s responsibility if we can’t rehouse him, and we can’t. He’s an old boy in his 70s. He’s so nervous about losing his house, he’s lived on the farm his whole life.”

An EWR Co spokesman said: “We understand the concerns regarding Mr Hurrell’s tenant. We take the health and wellbeing of landowners and local communities very seriously, and we would welcome further discussions with him on this issue to consider a solution.”

The Development Consent Order process, which seeks the Transport Secretary’s powers to compulsorily acquire land, should be completed and submitted to the Government by 2027, after which construction would begin. The aim is to complete the whole route by the mid-2030s.

That’s still years to wait for locals. “And that’s the big problem,” says Banks. “You’re left in a position of not knowing. You know it’s going to happen; clearly, it’s going to happen because the Government loves Cambridge, as it earns them money.

“And we’re stuck in this situation. We don’t know what they’re going to take or what they’re going to give back. It’s the classic case of you can’t move offices and go somewhere else. That doesn’t work here. It’s our home and our business and our children’s and my ancestors’ land and home. You’re in limbo.”

data.title: Heathrow to fine drivers £80 for overly long farewells
data.description:
People who take too long to bid farewell to loved ones at Heathrow face an £80 fine for overstaying their welcome.

A new 10-minute limit for unloading bags and wishing passengers a safe journey at Britain’s busiest airport will leave no time for drawn-out goodbyes.

The cap will be introduced for drivers at Heathrow’s forecourts from January as part of a shake-up that will push drop-off charges up from £6 to £7 in an inflation-busting 17pc increase.

Unlike most other major airports in the UK, Heathrow has not previously sought to limit the time people can spend dropping off friends, family and business colleagues.

However, hubs such as Gatwick and Luton allow over-stayers to pay by the minute rather than hitting them with a hefty fine.

Clive Wratten, the chief executive of the Business Travel Association, a lobby group, said the higher fees and strict time limit risk punishing passengers unnecessarily.

He said: “There’s next to no justification for this. These charges do nothing to improve the passenger experience or reliability of access to Heathrow. All it does is raise costs.”

Heathrow claimed that the 10-minute maximum stay, to be introduced across all of its terminal forecourts, would ease congestion and improve traffic flows.

It said: “This will enhance the customer experience for those using the terminal drop off as it is intended.”

It said 95pc of drop-offs take less than 10 minutes and that the time limit is expected to have a “minimal impact”.

A spokesman added: “Most people just drop off and go, they don’t hang around. It’s very busy, and we need to move people on. There just isn’t space in those terminal forecourts.”

One response to the scheme, however, could be for drivers who need more than 10 minutes to circle round and return to the drop-off area, accruing a £14 fee rather than the £80 fine, potentially adding to congestion.

Heathrow said the £7 basic charge – also backed up by an £80 fine for non-payment, reduced to £40 if settled within 14 days – brings it into line with other airports and is aimed partly at encouraging “options for travel that have a lower environmental impact”.

While Gatwick already charges £7 for the first 10 minutes on its forecourts, people can stay for longer at a cost of £1 per minute, up to a maximum of 30 minutes and £27.

Luton operates a similar system, charging £7 for 10 minutes and £1 per minute thereafter, again with a maximum stay of 30 minutes.

Manchester and Stansted airports have somewhat lower basic charges but a flat £25 rate for people who extend their time in the drop-off areas.

Heathrow said blue-badge holders will be exempt from the maximum stay limit and will still be able to use drop-off zones for free, so long as they register their trip.

data.title: Coaches boycott floating bus stop over safety fears
data.description:
A £4m floating bus stop built to encourage cycling has been boycotted by coaches over safety fears.

Shropshire county council ripped out two carriageways near Shrewsbury railway station to create a single lane for cars and a segregated bicycle lane in what it described as a “once in a generation opportunity” to promote walking and cycling.

But within weeks of its launch in October, Arriva Midlands revealed that a safety review of the bus stop meant its drivers would “no longer call at this stop”.

The Telegraph has established that the bus company boycott followed several “near misses” between cyclists and pedestrians and bus passengers.

Residents and businesses have also complained that the stop has caused gridlock on the medieval town’s one-way gyratory.

Floating bus stops, also known as “bus stop boarders” – where passengers have to cross a cycle lane to board or disembark the bus – have proven highly controversial amid claims vulnerable people, including the disabled and visually impaired, are forced to “run the gauntlet” of those cyclists who refuse to stop at zebra crossings.

Council documents reveal how the Shrewsbury stop was meant to promote “active travel”, but led to “regular reports of long queues”, businesses complaining that customers had “missed appointments” and 999 emergency response vehicles being stranded in traffic.

It also emerged that the design does not meet national standards because it is narrower than the Department for Transport guidelines.

Council reports catalogue the “public backlash” over the design, including how more than 6,600 people signed a petition calling for the road to return to two lanes for all traffic.

The files add how a “breakdown in communication” and determination to secure active travel “grant eligibility” culminated in the council becoming “committed to building something that hadn’t been through a full design process”.

The fire chief also gave local authority bosses a video of a “trial run” recording of a “fire engine driving in response through the gyratory system” which gave “a good indication of the potential challenges within the railway station area”.

Meanwhile, some fire appliances have had to stray into the cycle lane to get past traffic, raising concerns that cyclists wearing headphones could be at risk.

Julian Dean, a Green and Progressive Independents Group councillor on Shropshire council, said the cycle lane “was almost designed to wind people up and make them be against the active travel scheme”.

Council reports highlight how there are now fears about “risks of potential grant clawback” that could affect the council’s financial reserves.

The authority has launched an independent safety review to establish, among other things, whether there can be safe use of the floating bus stop.

Andrew Hooper, 37, who owns the Tap and Can bar and restaurant near the station, brewed a special “Panic at the Bus Stop” beer to mark the chaos the town has endured.

“It’s an utterly insane bus stop which has caused havoc and posed a safety risk to people,” he said, adding how his special beer sold out within five days.

An Arriva Midlands spokesman said it carried out a safety review of the bus stop, adding: “Based on our findings, our services will no longer call at this stop and we have informed the local authority and our customers.

“Our priority is always the safety of our passengers, colleagues and other road users and we’ll continue working with partners to identify a safe and workable solution.”

A council spokesman stressed how the council itself has not suspended the stop, adding how it was working with all parties “to find a workable solution”.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Trains risk being cancelled in heavy rain amid landslide fear
data.description:
Commuters across Britain face mass train cancellations during periods of heavy rain.

It comes after an investigation found that monitors failed to detect a landslide that caused a train to derail last month.

A spokesman for Network Rail said that the response to heavy downpour “has to be a local decision” taken by operators – but that cancelling trains outright is an option in the wake of the crash.

The high-speed Avanti West Coast service came off the tracks in Shap, Cumbria, in November, injuring four people.

The Rail Accident Investigation Branch (RAIB) said on Friday that equipment had been swallowed up by the landslip, providing no warning before an express train hit the debris.

It issued an emergency safety bulletin ordering Network Rail to take urgent steps to “mitigate” the risk to trains in light of the equipment failure.

It is understood that measures could include cancelling services during or ahead of extreme weather events, as well as increasing lineside checks and monitoring by Network Rail’s own inspection trains.

Concern about landslides has already seen services cancelled in the wake of the accident last month.

Northern, Britain’s second-biggest train operator, scrapped services across Cumbria, Lancashire and North Yorkshire on Monday after the Met Office issued an amber weather warning and Network Rail said 20cm of rain was expected in 48 hours on some routes.

Northern suffered its own near miss in March 2024 at Grange-over-Sands in Cumbria when the track collapsed ahead of a train as an embankment was washed away.

Avanti West Coast derailment

The RAIB said its investigation showed that monitors mounted on steel spikes every two metres along the base of the slope had detected minor earth movements four hours before the accident in Shap – but they were below the level needed to trigger a so-called green alert.

While movements continued for the next two hours, they stayed within the minimum range.

The landslide occurred at around 4.30am without ever triggering the higher amber, red and black alerts, with the two sensors immediately in its path “tipped over and subsumed by the material sliding down the slope,” the investigator said.

“It would appear that this occurred too quickly for them to determine and transmit their movement and to generate an alert,” it said.

“The sensors’ wireless signal was also unable to pass through the layer of material which covered them. This is based on them being able to re-establish a connection and report a variety of alert levels as the site was cleared.”

The RAIB said that, given the equipment failure, train operators and infrastructure providers should no longer assume that they would have early warning of future incidents.

It said: “Lineside monitoring equipment used on Network Rail-managed infrastructure may not be able to detect the failure of slopes in some circumstances.

“As a result, this equipment may not provide data as expected to support safety decision-making, particularly during extreme weather conditions.”

The 4.28am train from Glasgow Central to London Euston was travelling at 83mph – 40mph below its top speed – when it struck the landslip near Shap Summit, the RAIB said.

Debris lifted the front wheels of the Avanti service off the rails, derailing it – but the train ploughed on for another 560 metres before coming to a halt.

The investigation found that the landslip was caused after a drainage channel overflowed in a downpour, leading the slope below it to become saturated and later give way.

The RAIB also said that while the sensors were sending data to an online monitoring service, they had not been commissioned for operational use and so were not providing alerts to Network Rail’s control centre.

Network Rail said the derailment had exposed a “new and previously unknown failure mechanism” where sensors get quickly buried and the mobile phone signal used to transmit alerts cannot get through.

It said engineers were working with suppliers to understand the issue and strengthen the performance of the sensors, which had provided useful data on landslips in the past.

It added: “We have one of the safest railways in the world and the safety of passengers and our colleagues remains our first priority.”

The weather is one of the most common reasons given by train companies for delays and cancellations of services.

In August, South Western Railway said the “driest conditions for around 200 years” forced it to halve the amount of trains that run from London Waterloo to Exeter St Davids.

data.title: The lobby uncovers Labour sleaze – which is why Starmer wants it muzzled
data.description:
It is easy to be annoyed by the lobby, the small band of political journalists given privileged access to Parliament and government briefings. I say this as someone who was a member of that band for 16 years. The lobby can be shallow, self-regarding and sometimes silly. But the fact that the people it annoys most are the people with power tells you all you need to know about how important it is.

This is the context for the Starmer Government’s attempt to curtail the lobby and its journalists – and why I suspect those efforts will fail, just as previous ones did.

The push is being driven by Tim Allan, the Prime Minister’s head of communications. Allan wants to reduce the number of briefings held each week between Downing Street and the lobby. These briefings are the core of the system. They involve the Prime Minister’s official spokesman fielding questions from up to 50 political correspondents on absolutely anything.

Even if you have never heard of the lobby and have no interest in it, you absorb the results of its work every day. Almost every headline about what the Government is doing is shaped, in part, by what emerges from the lobby briefing. In that sense, it is an essential part of democratic life.

From a Downing Street perspective, however, it is simply a pain in the backside. The briefings absorb time and resource, with officials required to prepare the spokesman to field a barrage of sharply pointed questions from all angles.

More importantly, the lobby makes it hard to control the story. Control is what communications professionals crave: deciding what gets talked about and how. The lobby is fundamentally uncontrollable.

Unhelpful stories frequently emerge from the amicable chaos of a lobby briefing. Political correspondents of my vintage recall 2013 headlines about David Cameron supposedly telling elderly people worried about energy bills to put on another jumper. The Prime Minister had said no such thing, but a slightly hapless spokesman was manoeuvred into a quotable remark by feral journalists hunting a story.

More seriously, before and after the Iraq war of 2003, statements made by Tony Blair’s spokesmen in lobby briefings became central to the Hutton inquiry into the death of weapons scientist David Kelly and the wider debate about the honesty of the government’s case for war. Every line and inference mattered. Lobby briefings were then potentially matters of war and peace, life and death.

That period is crucial to understanding the current attempt to clip the lobby’s wings. Alastair Campbell, then Blair’s communications chief, made the first serious effort to dismantle it. In 2002, he announced that regular lobby briefings would be scrapped and replaced with on-camera briefings featuring ministers and senior officials. A more transparent, American-style system dominated by broadcasters, he claimed, would be more democratic and open.

Campbell – like Allan today – was seeking an arrangement that would allow the government to present itself in the best possible light while facing the least possible scrutiny. With all due respect to broadcasters, televised briefings entail far less sustained pressure than off-camera Westminster encounters. In a press conference, a journalist might get one question and a brief follow-up before the agenda moves on.

In a lobby briefing, questioning can be sustained again and again on the same point, without concern for how it looks on camera. The government representative is placed under far greater pressure than when required to answer a couple of questions before smiling and calling the next journalist.

Allan, who worked for Campbell in the 1990s, is attempting to complete his old master’s work. He, to be fair, is a responding to a changing media landscape in which newspapers command a smaller share of public attention, with more time devoted to broadcasters, short-form video and online “content creators”.

But anyone who believes the lobby has lost relevance should reflect on political news in recent years. The resignations of Angela Rayner and Peter Mandelson would not have happened without the work of lobby journalists. Nor would the public have learnt about Boris Johnson’s lockdown parties in Number 10.

The idea that switching to on-camera briefings will give the government more control may also be optimistic. Campbell’s experiment ended spectacularly in 2002 when Admiral Sir Michael Boyce, then chief of the defence staff, was sent to a press conference to explain how well the Armed Forces were being supported. Instead, he explained – live on television – that the military’s capability of fighting a war in Iraq had been undermined by using almost 20,000 troops to replace striking firefighters. The embarrassment helped bring the experiment to an end.

The other awkward fact is that even if you reduce the number of lobby briefings, the lobby will not go away – nor will its questions. Downing Street sometimes dreams of emulating the White House’s televised daily briefings – overlooking the fact that American correspondents also speak regularly off-camera to those in power.

So by all means, scrap the lobby briefings and conjure up some risky on-camera sessions. But you will never be rid of journalists doing the basic job of journalism: asking difficult questions of those who govern. Long live the lobby.
