data.title: Britain’s highest-paid woman earns £280m in a year from gambling empire
data.description:
Britain’s highest-paid woman has taken home at least £280m despite a sharp drop in profits at her gambling empire.

Denise Coates, the billionaire owner of Bet365, paid herself a salary of £104m in the year to the end of March, up from £95m in 2024.

In addition, Ms Coates’s majority stake in the company means she is entitled to at least half of the £353.6m dividend paid out over the year, taking her total pay packet to at least £280m.

The payout marks an increase on the £158m the 58-year-old took home last year but is short of her record £469m pay deal in 2021.

Ms Coates has generated a vast fortune from Bet365, the gambling giant she launched online from a portable cabin in Stoke-on-Trent in 2000.

She took control of the company from her father, Peter, who set up the business as a high street betting chain. Ms Coates now holds a 58pc stake.

Ms Coates and her family ranked 16th on the latest Sunday Times Rich List with an estimated fortune of £9.45bn.

The latest payday came even as Bet365’s pre-tax profits slumped almost by half to £348.7m, down from £626.6m the previous year.

The decline was driven by almost £325m in administrative expenses as the company expanded into new markets in the US and South America. Bet365 also ended its controversial presence in China, an unregulated market where gambling is banned.

Revenues stood at just over £4bn, up 9pc from £3.7bn, according to newly-filed accounts.

It follows reports earlier this year that Ms Coates was eyeing up a £9bn sale of Bet365. The tycoon is said to have held discussions with Wall Street banks and advisers about a potential deal, which could include listing all or part of the business on a US stock exchange or selling a stake to a private equity investor.

Any deal would allow the Coates family to further cash in on the success of their business empire. A full sale could land Ms Coates a payday of more than £5bn.

Meanwhile, a sale would also provide an opportunity for an American buyer to capitalise on the rapid growth of gambling in the US after a 2018 Supreme Court decision that relaxed decades-old laws banning sports betting.

While Ms Coates has attracted scrutiny for her vast remuneration, supporters have noted that she has always paid herself in the UK and through a salary, rather than solely via more tax-efficient dividends, making her one of the country’s biggest taxpayers.

The company also donated £130m to her charity, the Denise Coates Foundation, last year.

Ms Coates, who lives in a £90m mansion in Cheshire, stays out of the spotlight. However, she is a patron of the arts and has rooms at the Courtauld Gallery in London named after her. The Coates family also owns Stoke City Football Club.

Bet365 waived loans to the football club, which is now under the control of Ms Coates’s brother, John, following a demerger last year.

Over the past two decades, Peter Coates and companies within the Bet365 group have also given hundreds of thousands of pounds to Labour.

data.title: Poundland to tap emergency overdraft after Christmas trading slump
data.description:
Struggling discount chain Poundland is preparing to call on millions of pounds in emergency financing to combat a worrying slump in trading.

The retailer will soon begin drawing down on a £30m overdraft granted by its previous owners, Pepco.

The Polish company agreed to provide the capital in a last-ditch deal to offload the business to new investors.

The chain was rescued in the summer by distressed investment specialist Gordon Brothers as part of a court-sanctioned restructuring plan that paved the way for it to close scores of loss-making stores.

It reported that the Boston-based investment firm acquired Poundland for £1, a deal that protected most of the 16,000 staff employed across 825 UK stores.

Under the terms of the agreement, Pepco agreed to provide its former subsidiary with substantial funds, including an immediate £30m loan and a separate £30m debt tranche in the form of an overdraft.

Gordon Brothers subsequently shut two of Poundland’s warehouses and 68 of its worst-performing stores in a push to boost the retailer’s profitability, putting more than 2,000 jobs at risk.

However, Poundland’s struggles have intensified amid a broader slump in festive sales across the sector.

New figures from Sensormatic show that store visits across the UK high street were down 13pc year-on-year on Dec 23, typically one of the busiest shopping days of the year.

Retailers are also now anticipating a further drop in sales in January, according to a survey from the Confederation of British Industry, which revealed bosses’ expectations are at their weakest level since March 2021.

Gordon Brothers informed Pepco of its wish to draw down on the credit facility in recent weeks as trading fell short of forecasts, creating a liquidity crunch.

Poundland plans to access the emergency funding in two stages, drawing down its first instalment in January before reaccessing it later this year.

Pepco is understood to have initially resisted the request, raising fresh concerns about Poundland’s survival prospects.

However, an agreement was reached at a board meeting, alleviating concerns about its immediate future.

A phalanx of advisers has overseen the proposals. Gordon Brothers drafted in a team of forensic accountants from AlixPartners to carefully monitor Poundland’s cash flow shortly after taking over in July.

Poundland’s board has enlisted its own specialist corporate financiers from FRP Advisory to observe performance.

The budget chain is on track to close around 130 stores by next February under the restructuring plan implemented by Gordon Brothers.

In stores earmarked for closure, clearance sales have been launched across a wide range of stock, with reductions of up to 40pc.

Earlier this week, Poundland announced it will not open on Christmas Day, Boxing Day or New Year’s Day, continuing an established practice of prioritising staff wellbeing over sales.

A spokesman for Poundland said: “The restructuring plan has unlocked the financial support we needed, giving us more than enough headroom to implement our recovery plans. We continue to receive the full support of Gordon Brothers and our former owner, Pepco.”

“Our stores are in the best shape they’ve been in for some time – well stocked, strongly merchandised and serving customers.”

“While, of course, there remains much to do, we are pleased with progress in the last few months, as we get the business back on track.”

Pepco declined to comment.

data.title: Jenny Seagrove: ‘I’ve not always made the right choices, but who cares?’
data.description:
Despite early screen success in The Woman in White (1982), Local Hero (1983) and Diana (1984), Jenny Seagrove too often felt trapped in genteel English rose roles at the start of her career. Yet life – and opportunities – changed overnight when she was cast as steely Yorkshire housemaid turned international business tycoon Emma Harte in Eighties rags-to-riches TV miniseries, A Woman of Substance, based on Barbara Taylor Bradford’s novel.

With her northern brogue, working-class roots and fierce ambition, Taylor Bradford’s ballsy heroine – who escaped poverty at the turn of the 20th century to build a global empire against the odds – was so far from Seagrove’s usual repertoire it was a “pinch me” moment that still resonates 40 years later.

We’re talking as the landmark drama returns to Channel 4 this weekend, before a new adaptation of the saga, launching next year, with Outlander’s Jessica Reynolds and Vera’s Brenda Blethyn in the leading roles of young and old Emma. Modern classics are invariably reworked every few years for a new generation, but this has particularly big boots to fill. Not only did the sweeping original feature film star Deborah Kerr and John Mills – as well as notable early outings by Liam Neeson and Miranda Richardson – but it also drew just shy of 14 million viewers in 1985, still a record audience for Channel 4.

“It was so weird,” recalls Seagrove of her initial destiny-laden encounter with the 1979 bestseller. “I’d been making a film in New Zealand and I was at that point in my career when I thought, ‘I must buy some rights to books so that I can set up my own production company’. I saw A Woman of Substance at the airport on my journey home, read it on the plane and instantly thought, ‘No one’s going to cast me as a working-class Yorkshire girl’. But then I got a phone call from my agent... And the rest is history.”

“It was liberating. I’ve played a huge selection of parts, but in this business you get pigeonholed and people go: ‘Oh, English rose’, so it was wonderful that they took a risk on me – and that the risk paid off.”

Filming took place on location around Aysgarth and Richmond, North Yorkshire, as well as in London, with Harrods doubling for the fictional Harte’s store, and exteriors featuring, among other landmarks, the Ritz Hotel.

“I just loved it,” fizzes Seagrove, 68, her soulful blue eyes suddenly dancing at the memory. “I was playing this marvellous woman going from 16 through to 50-something, surrounded by wonderful people. I was just having a blast. I probably had the chutzpah of youth, you’ve no time to feel overawed. You just dive in.”

Seagrove felt an instant affinity with the character of Harte despite their contrasting backgrounds. The actress was born in Kuala Lumpur, where her father ran an import-export firm. When she was less than a year old, her mother suffered a stroke leaving her weak and unable to stand for long periods. At the age of nine, Seagrove was shipped back to the UK to attend boarding school in Surrey, before training at Bristol Old Vic.

 

“I’ve always been inspired by women who create their own narrative, and Emma absolutely did that. In life, you can’t wait for people to give you opportunities. You have to go out there and work for them; she made things happen. I do know that the drama changed lives. For many years, I’d get people stopping me, saying, ‘I started my own business’. It inspired women to follow their dream.”

Appropriately, a sequel, Hold the Dream, followed a year later, also starring Seagrove (as Emma’s granddaughter, a role played by Richardson in A Woman of Substance) cementing close bonds with Taylor Bradford – who died last year, aged 91 – as well as US performer and producer Diane Baker. “The first time I met Barbara was at the screening [for A Woman of Substance] when she said, ‘You are my Emma Harte’. That meant more than anything else,” says Seagrove. “I went over [to America] for her 90th birthday, and then to her funeral, and I did the memorial service for her in London.”

On the back of the show’s success, Seagrove found herself considered for a raft of sought-after roles, yet what should have been a golden time proved less so in hindsight.

“It took me global, which was lovely, but I had a very strange relationship with fame, and did I make the right career decision afterwards?” she muses. “No, I don’t think I did, but my career has been filled with all sorts of things, none of which I regret. I’ve done so much and worked with so many fascinating people, but have I always made the right choices? Absolutely not, but who cares?”

Continuing on the theme, she concedes: “Sometimes I took things I shouldn’t have and I turned some things down. Sometimes it’s as simple as who I was with. At that time in my life, there were influences on my career from my private life, and you can’t legislate these things. You can’t tell your heart not to do what it wants to do.”

In 1984, she married the Indian actor Madhav Sharma, but it ended in divorce in 1988. She subsequently had a relationship with the flamboyant film director Michael Winner, which stoked the gossip columns for several years. “I don’t believe in regrets. If I hadn’t gone through them [the relationships], I wouldn’t be who I am now, and I’m at peace with myself. So you learn from them, I wouldn’t change anything.”

Seagrove is refreshingly honest. “I’ve never really known who I am. None of us grow from good things. We all grow from pain. And so therefore I’ve always sort of hated nasty things happening, but welcomed them at the same time, and just known that I could get through them. I was amazed to find out quite how resilient I was. We all have to learn to love ourselves, and once you can actually accept who you are, warts and all, then I think you can start to be liberated. I’ve sort of accepted who I am. I know I’m flawed. I know my failings.”

In 1994 she embarked on a relationship with the theatre producer and Everton FC chairman Bill Kenwright, which endured until his death two years ago, aged 78. It was the partnership she’d been seeking, a soulmate in all senses. They collaborated on countless theatre productions over the years, often with Kenwright’s long-time friend – and Seagrove’s former Judge John Deed co-star – Martin Shaw.

Kenwright’s loss has been devastating for Seagrove. She could have backed away and grieved quietly for the man who shared her life for nearly three decades, yet in a move worthy of Harte, she stepped up to lead Kenwright’s company as chairman. In some ways it’s been her salvation.

“It’s kept me busy at a time when I could have just sat and looked at four walls,” she admits. “It’s been incredibly helpful, as well as incredibly stressful. I’ve had to learn quickly, but I have the most marvellous team here. I feel that they’re my family. The last two years have also been about finding out who I am, because when you’re in a relationship you see yourself as a partner, and now I’m on my own. It’s been about adapting.”

She’s making progress on that score, turning a corner this year by accepting two acting jobs, and is currently planning a theatre tour.

“Ken and I were very different people. He was a huge character, very brave. I’m a quieter person, but we both shared the same loves. I taught him about the love of animals. He taught me to love Everton Football Club. There was a deep sharing of everything and a respect for each other.”

Seagrove, who is a proud animal rights campaigner, finds joy in the day-to-day walking her springer spaniel (she and Kenwright moved out of London in the pandemic) and working for her horse rescue charity, Mane Chance. “Those are all things that get you out of bed when you don’t necessarily want to. It’s the hour before dawn where the brain goes to a dark place... Once you’re up, you’re having to think: ‘Now, where did I put the tea bags?’”

While she welcomes distraction, Seagrove draws the line at watching herself on TV – so she won’t be streaming A Woman of Substance alongside fans, nostalgic for an era that spawned other glamorous business shows such as Dallas and Dynasty. “I haven’t seen it for years,” she laughs. “I always think I could do better, and I just can’t bear watching myself.”

And as for the new version in the pipeline, she has mixed feelings. “I wish them luck, and part of me is very excited for Barbara’s brand; she wanted the remake for a whole new generation. But another part of me, honestly, is quite sad, because it’ll take the reins from where mine was. But that’s life. If you hand the baton on, which you have to, it means that the story gets told all over again to a new audience, so that has to be a good thing.”

So would she have fancied a crack at playing Emma again? “It’s a question that really is irrelevant because they didn’t ask me!” she retorts. “If the script was wonderful, I think I’d have probably gone, ‘Oh, how lovely.’ But with Brenda Blethyn playing older Emma, I think there’ll be a twinkle. Emma’s got to have a twinkle.”

The 1985 series of A Woman of Substance is on Channel4.com from Sunday, December 21.

data.title: Labour’s interference in the property market will slow the economy
data.description:
SIR – Successive governments have chipped away at the attraction of the private rental market (“The influencers coaching tenants to fight their landlords”, report, December 20). By giving tenants security of tenure and limiting landlords’ ability to regain possession, the Renters’ Rights Act 2025 has accelerated landlords’ exit from the market. 

How long before the Government is pressured into extending the limited restrictions contained in the 2025 Act to full-on rent controls, putting the final nail in the coffin of labour mobility? 

Here we have yet another instance of a government talking “growth” and achieving the opposite.

Michael Hodgson
Petersfield, Hampshire

SIR – As a minor private landlord, I am no friend of this Labour Government, which seeks to pile more burdens on those of us who use retirement funds to provide homes to rent. However, the previous Tory administration is also to blame, having turned ownership of rental property into a minefield of regulations and increased risk. 

I have decided that the way forward is to put money into a real estate investment trust that gives similar or better financial returns. This frees me from exposure to a dozen or more possible prosecutions, as well as significantly reducing my workload – but unfortunately my tenants will have to find new homes.

Jonathan Carr
Shrewsbury

SIR – Sam Brodbeck’s article, “The attack on our property has only just begun” (Money, December 6), is spot on. Prices are so inflated that young buyers are locked out; sales have stalled; landlords are fleeing; and the old promise of capital gains has been taxed away. A property dégringolade now looks inevitable.

However, this may be strategy, not drift. Labour knows that older, equity-rich owners – once Conservative, now drifting to Reform UK – are not its voters. But a whole generation suddenly able to buy could be. Driving prices down through tax and regulation would deliver that prize.

There will be anguish around dinner tables – but votes for Labour.

Ronald Lansdell
St Helier, Jersey

SIR – We have a one-bedroom bungalow in a wardened retirement complex near Basingstoke, which has been on the market since May 2024, now priced into negative equity. The estate agent’s excuse is that it is a “niche market”.

Since then, it has cost us more than £16,000 in council tax, service charges, ground rent and refurbishments. Where are all the pensioners desperate for smaller accommodation?

Isabel Murray
Nursling, Hampshire
 

Starmer on China

SIR – I totally agree with Charles Moore’s condemnation of the way Jimmy Lai, a British citizen, is being treated in Hong Kong (“The conviction of Jimmy Lai shames Britain”, Comment, December 16).

I lived in Hong Kong in the 1980s, when the Sino-British Joint Declaration was being negotiated, and we were led to believe that the “One Country, Two Systems” principle would be respected for 50 years. My late wife was adamant that it would not be honoured by the Chinese government – and how right she was. 

We now have a British citizen likely to serve a lengthy prison sentence for daring to speak out. I put it to Sir Keir Starmer that here is a real chance to regain a modicum of public respect by protesting in the strongest possible terms regarding the treatment of Jimmy Lai when he visits China early in the new year.

It is time we stood four square against this outrage. The Government needs to stick up for the people of this nation instead of fawning over leaders of countries that do not live by the same democratic values.

John Firrell
Litton Cheney, Dorset

SIR – In light of the improper imprisonment of Jimmy Lai in Hong Kong, could our Government suggest to the Chinese that further consideration of the building of their new embassy in London will be on hold until he is released?

Tom Boyd-Smith
Norton-on-Tees, Co Durham
 

Foreign aid worth

SIR – I read with interest your report on foreign aid (“Foreign aid bankrolls hotel and golf trips”, December 12).

I proudly work as a contracted consultant for the UK Trade Partnerships Programme, which is funded by the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office. I am often contracted on a project-by-project basis by the International Trade Centre, which runs the programme, and is rigorous when it comes to expenses and travel. It is certainly not a “gravy train”, as Richard Tice, deputy leader of Reform UK, puts it, and I take my responsibilities very seriously when I know that my contractor fee is paid by the taxpayer.

The programme helps developing countries use trade deals to boost exports to the UK, and creates jobs and growth through technical help and capacity building, reducing poverty as well as benefitting British consumers. I hope that Mr Tice would agree that these are good things.

Gerald Lawson-Tancred
Thirsk, North Yorkshire
 

Rap revelation

SIR – With reference to recent discussion of rap music (“Chalamet video stops rumours he is masked rapper EsDeeKid”, report, December 21), I was hired by a chiropractor to write a parody of Baby Got Back, a rap track by Sir Mix-a-Lot. Until then, my view of rap was that it was just noise. This is no longer the case. 

The track has 117 non-repeating lines with most difficult timing. Writing the parody to match the phrasing was a real challenge. I tried to record it with the backing track, but could never do it in one take, such was the rapid delivery necessary. 

As with all types of music, the rap genre should not be dismissed on the basis of a few encounters. Though it does not appeal to me, I now appreciate that there is real artistry involved. 

Keith Ougden 
Paphos, Cyprus
 

The NHS’s fatal flaw

SIR – The first step in abolishing the NHS (Letters, December 22) is to educate the public about the expense of medical treatment. 

Since the creation of the NHS in 1948, generations of people have come to believe that free healthcare is a right, such that many are prepared to spend thousands on a cruise but not a hip replacement.

Nye Bevan naively believed that the NHS would improve general health to such an extent as to make itself practically redundant. In fact, clean air and water, along with immunisation programmes, have done more than hospital care ever has. 

Even when I qualified in 1978, medical wards were full of the elderly at the end of their lives. Today, though we may live longer, we do so with failing organs and joints, the management of which was not dreamed of by the founding fathers – and which is very expensive.

The only way forward is an insurance-based scheme.

David Nunn FRCS
West Malling, Kent
 

Lords reform

SIR – An elected House of Lords would be a disaster (Letters, December 16). Far from reflecting the wishes of the nation, it would be subject to the vagaries of public opinion, rather than acting as a steadying hand on the tiller of the Lower House. A glance across the Atlantic shows the problems inherent in such an arrangement.

Chris Benn
Swayfield, Lincolnshire

SIR – Perhaps a new Upper House should be populated by randomly selected members of the voting public on a three-month cycle. There will be no shortage of individuals to take up these posts if David Lammy goes ahead with judge-only trials.

Graeme Brierley
Sutton Bridge, Lincolnshire
 

Leaving a mark

SIR – Your report (December 5) that rangers in the Lake District have pleaded with visitors to stop carving their names into tree trunks reminded me of a tree I came across in a local wood several years ago. 

There were many carvings in it, but only one was fully legible, showing three initials and “Texas 1944”. I have often wondered what happened to the men who carved their names into that tree so far from home.

John Newbury
Warminster, Wiltshire
 

The legacy of British colonial rule in Malta

SIR – I read with interest the article on colonial rule by Kapil Komireddi (Comment, December 19).

Malta, my country of origin, was liberated by Britain from oppressive Napoleonic occupation in 1800. It benefited from British administration and later became a Crown Colony, until independence in 1964.

The island’s allegiance to the Crown was at its strongest during the Second World War, when it was nearly annihilated by the Axis forces. Britain suffered great losses defending it. The Maltese people’s valour earned the island the George Cross in 1942, which remains emblazoned on the national flag.

Britain has left an indelible mark on the Maltese people, who adopted English as their second language, commonly used in public services and education. Although there have been regrettable altercations, including the tragedy of forces shooting hungry protesters, the overall benefit to the people of Malta is felt today in their daily lives.

Malta is now a prosperous tourist hot-spot. British holidaymakers can still see an occasional red phone box, red postboxes and a grand statue of Queen Victoria in Valletta.

History is what made us and should never be cancelled.

Dr René Tayar
Tadworth, Surrey
 

Getting to grips with a bloated Civil Service

SIR – Reform UK’s pledge to scrap almost 70,000 civil servants sounds impressive until one realises just how many of them there are (“If cutting civil servants were easy, it would have been done years ago”, Comment, December 16). 

The Ministry of Defence alone employs about 60,000 – more than the combined military personnel of the Royal Navy and Royal Air Force. With many of these people working from home for much of the week, and services such as HMRC and the Land Registry now measuring their response times in months or years where once it was days or weeks, I doubt that we would notice a 25 per cent reduction. A round of job cuts in certain departments might even inspire some to up their game to avoid being laid off.

Neil Russell
Plymouth, Devon

SIR – It is alarming to read that the number of civil servants went up by 62,000 in a single year (Money, December 19). 

These employees certainly aren’t making Britain a better, more competitive place to live. Sadly, the same mentality exists in parish and town councils. 

Meanwhile, taxes are put up to pay their wages. 

Richard Cussons 
Knutsford, Cheshire
 

Letters to the Editor

We accept letters by email and post. Please include name, address, work and home telephone numbers. 
ADDRESS: 111 Buckingham Palace Road, London, SW1W 0DT 
EMAIL: dtletters@telegraph.co.uk 
FOLLOW: Telegraph Letters @LettersDesk

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Inflation calculator: how the latest rate will affect your savings
data.description:
This calculator shows the current rate of Consumer Prices Index (CPI) inflation will affect the return on your savings.

Inflation calculator

How to use this calculator

Type in the amount you have in a savings account or cash Isa, and the interest rate you’re currently receiving.

Press the “Calculate” button to see the result.

How it works

This calculator works by figuring out how much interest your savings will earn over the course of a year – this assumes interest is paid annually, and the interest rate and amount of savings remains the same.

This is pitted against the most recent CPI measure of inflation, as reported by the Office for National Statistics.

Assuming inflation remains steady, it then shows the value your savings will lose in real terms due to inflationary price increases.

The calculator will also show you what the cumulative impact of these losses will be for each of the next five years.

What is this calculator useful for?

The calculator can help you gauge whether or not it’s worth switching to a savings account or cash Isa offering a higher rate of interest.

This is particularly important when inflation is high, but in general it’s a good idea to make sure you’re taking advantage of the best savings rates to reduce the effect of price increases. If you don’t, put simply, your money won’t buy as much as it could the previous year.

You can also use the calculator to see how much of a difference it would make to switch savings accounts for a better rate.

For example, someone with £20,000 in an account paying today’s average easy-access rate, according to Moneyfacts, of 2.56pc would make a “real terms” loss of £124.03 after a year because of inflation. They would earn £512 in interest, but their pot would effectively be worth just £19,875.97 after a year when adjusted for inflation.

Switching to the top easy-access savings rate which, according to today’s tables showing the best easy access savings accounts, is from Cahoot and pays 5pc, they’d make an inflationary increase of £348.84, based on actual interest of £1,000.

data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”

data.title: Locals sleep in 1950s bus shelter to stop council demolishing it
data.description:
Residents of a seaside town are camping out in their “cherished” 1950s bus shelter to stop a council demolishing it.

Demonstrators in Sheringham, Norfolk, have been braving the cold weather to protect the structure from demolition since Dec 1.

Norfolk county council wants to knock down the brick shelter outside the North Norfolk Railway Station and replace it with a modern glass and steel “hub”.

But for residents, the shelter, which showcases a mural from local artist Colin Seal, is a “cherished” part of the town’s identity that needs to be saved.

The group are considering continuing their sit-in on Christmas Day.

Save Sheringham’s Historic Bus Shelter, a campaign group, said: “We’ve lost track of the number of people we’ve spoken to who have expressed their support in so many ways, from Sheringham and beyond. This is growing, day by day, hour by hour.

“We want as many people as possible to come down between those hours to stand and support us. As with everything we’ve done so far, this is to be peaceful, respectful and lawful.”

The impromptu protest began when the workmen were spotted arriving at the scene on Monday afternoon and police were on-scene.

Since then, protesters have camped out all day and all night in shifts in front of the shelter to foil the council’s plans of demolishing the structure.

However, despite their efforts, the county council has said that it will not be backing down.

Instead it has asked for the protesters to “leave immediately” and argued that disrupting the works by entering a construction site is “dangerous” for everyone.

The council also reissued its stance on the issue when asked by this paper if it still intended to go ahead with tearing down the structure.

Katie Hart set up the original petition to get the bus shelter listed and has camped out since 6.30pm on Tuesday night.

She said: “The police came to question us. We thought ‘Can we get in here without causing damage?’

“They said a complaint has been put in and the contractors accused us of breaking a clip to get in here.

“We want to keep it peaceful. We are not here to antagonise. We are here to make a point to the council that no one is listening. Our own MP is asking them to halt.”

Lisa Woods, the part-owner of The Sheringham Trawler restaurant, brought chips out to the demonstrators.

She said: “It is the nostalgia of Sheringham. I think the plans are the worst thing that a lot of people have heard.”

A spokesman for the council said: “The concerns raised by some local residents are fully noted and have been considered throughout the development of the project, with many suggestions being incorporated into the design.

“But retaining the existing shelter is not a viable option and disrupting the works by entering a construction site is dangerous for all concerned.

“We want these works to be delivered as safely and quickly as possible so everyone living and working in or visiting Sheringham can benefit from this new amenity and we ask that those involved leave the area immediately.”

Lib Dem MP tables motion

Steff Aquarone, the North Norfolk MP, stood with the protesters in support at the shelter on Thursday. He said on a social media post: “When we stand together, we can create change. Conservative-run Norfolk county council must engage and listen, instead of ploughing ahead with their bullyboy approach.”

Mr Aquarone has also tabled an “early day motion”, which has gained the support of nine other Lib Dems, in a bid to get the matter discussed in Parliament.

It calls on the county council to suspend its plans to allow for further “constructive discussion with the community” and said: “Sheringham is a town which is not to be messed with.”

Norfolk Police said: “Officers were called to Station Approach, Sheringham at 9.45am (Dec 3) to a report of criminal damage to fencing surrounding a bus shelter.

“Initial inquiries were conducted, and no crime has been recorded.”

data.title: Norway strikes oil in North Sea near British waters
data.description:
Norway has announced a string of North Sea oil and gas discoveries close to UK waters.

British operators Harbour Energy and Aker BP have both made recent finds in the Norwegian North Sea close to the border with UK waters. It suggests that the region still has a wealth of potential even as Labour shuts down activity.

Norway’s Okea has discovered more oil in a field slightly closer to Norway. Meanwhile, a wildcat well drilled by state energy firm Equinor was also successful, discovering seven million barrels.

In a new report, the Norwegian Offshore Directorate described the North Sea as “the powerhouse of the Norwegian petroleum industry, with 69 fields in production”.

Norway has drilled around 45 exploratory wells in 2025, with 12 yielding commercial quantities of oil and gas. This included 30 in the North Sea of which six were economic.

Its discoveries are in stark contrast to the UK sector of the North Sea, which has been hit by the Government’s 78pc total tax rate on oil and gas profits and a ban on new exploration.

The energy levies means the UK industry is now in recession, contracting at around 15pc a year and losing 1,000 jobs a month according to trade body estimates.

In October, companies operating in UK waters produced oil and gas equivalent to 33 million barrels of oil, just a fifth what it was producing in 2000 and a quarter of Norway’s current monthly output of 126 million barrels.

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, argues that the UK’s North Sea is in inexorable decline and the country must break its reliance on fossil fuels. Some industry experts disagree, saying the UK will need oil and gas for decades to come and North Sea reserves could supply much of the country’s needs.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said: “Norway is creating jobs, investment, and tax revenue that we’re missing out on in Britain. Making ourselves more dependent on foreign imports while refusing to drill our own resources will be remembered as the biggest act of economic self-harm in a generation.”

In contrast to the British policy, Norway’s official oil and gas policy aims to “provide a framework for the profitable production of oil and gas in the long term”.

The latest discovery, by Harbour Energy last week, opened up a new gas condensate field about 125 miles north-east of Shetland. Another oil discovery by Aker BP lies just over the UK/Norwegian border.

Each of the new fields is relatively small, but together they boost Norway’s output cheaply because they can be linked to existing subsea pipelines and other infrastructure.

The Norwegian Offshore Directorate report makes clear that Norway’s sector of the North Sea will remain productive for years. Much of its gas and some of its oil is destined for the UK, replacing supplies lost by the UK Government deliberately running down its own sector.

Robin Allan, the chairman of the Association of British Independent Exploration Companies (Brindex), said the North Sea had enough oil and gas to support the UK for years or decades provided the Government changed its policies on tax and regulation.

He said: “The oil and gas reservoirs from which Norwegian oil and gas is extracted are the same as those in the UK, but the UK has a hostile policy with punitive, self-defeating taxes, meaning we have locked ourselves into reliance on oil and gas imports.”

Mike Tholen, of Offshore Energies, said: “The UK combines high tax rate rates with unpredictability, and that comes at a cost to investment. If the UK wants to ensure it has secure energy supplies and support jobs, it needs a tax regime that encourages investment, not one that drives it away.”

A spokesman for the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said it was committed to renewables and a move away from fossil fuels.

They said: “Issuing new licences to explore new fields will not take a penny off bills, cannot make us energy secure and will only accelerate the worsening climate crisis.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Ten things to do before retirement (or risk your pension falling short)
data.description:
For most of our working lives, retirement can feel like a theoretical concept. But if you have five years or less to go until you reach retirement age, it becomes more of a reality.

By that time, your financial position is more certain, and any financial forecasts can be more realistic. This is the key time to check that your plans really are on track and make final decisions.

Here, Telegraph Money explains the important steps to consider.

Five years before retirement

1. Check your state pension entitlement (and fill gaps if appropriate)

Confirm what state pension you will get, and when. The age at which it can be claimed is currently 66, but this is set to gradually rise to 67 between 2026 and 2028, which could catch out those expecting to start claiming next year.

Because of how the age rise is being phased in, it means someone turning 66 in January can start claiming from their 66th birthday, but those born later in the year might face a delay. For example, someone turning 66 on June 1 will have to wait until they are 66 and two months old to start claiming; someone with a 66th birthday on October 1 can only claim once they are 66 and six months old.

Check our state pension age calculator to find out when you can start your claim.

The amount you are paid is based on your own National Insurance record; it may not be the full rate (currently £11,973 a year), so check yours via the Government Gateway.

If you have gaps in your National Insurance record, it may be worth paying to fill them.

2. Decide whether to use the services of an adviser

Going it alone or using the services of a financial adviser is something you need to think about and plan for a few years before actually retiring.

Shop around and take your time when it comes to finding an IFA, said Ian Millward of Candid Financial Advice.

“Avoid anything too complex or someone who tries to blind you with science. You need trust – but that shouldn’t be blind trust – and you need to understand the plan. The relationship should feel collaborative.”

Alternatively, the government-backed agency Pension Wise provides free guidance to people from age 50, to help you understand your retirement options. It will tell you what you could do, not what you should do.

3. Review your investments and ‘lifestyling’ options

There is a tendency to reduce investment risk as you approach retirement. So, with just a few years to go, now is a good time to look at where your pensions are invested and consider if this is the right level of risk for you.

Some pensions have what is known as “lifestyling” built in, where stock market holdings are automatically reduced in favour of things such as gilts and cash in the years before your nominated retirement age.

However, should gilts crash – as they have in the past – this could leave your savings vulnerable. Plus, you could miss out on growth if you de-risk too early.

“A couple probably have a life expectancy of over 30 years between them [after reaching retirement age] and need the kind of return only the stock market can provide to give them a secure retirement.

“It may sound counter-intuitive but de-risking into retirement can actually be more risky, as being too cautious can lead to you running out of money in old age,” said Mr Millward.

Now may also be the time to consolidate your pensions, and have your savings in one place – but only where it makes sense.

Older schemes sometimes have benefits, such as enhanced levels of tax-free cash, guaranteed annuity rates or some old with-profits contracts providing minimum guaranteed returns. These benefits would be lost on transfer.

4. Boost pension savings – it’s not too late

Ideally, by this time you’ve saved enough throughout your working life to build a pension pot that can sustain you throughout retirement. However, life can be messy and sometimes it can actually make sense to make larger contributions later in life.

“Often this will be when children are no longer dependent, the mortgage has been paid off and salary is highest,” said Tom Selby, of AJ Bell.

“This is one reason why annual pension contribution limits need to be high and ‘carry forward rules’ are important – it provides flexibility to make up for lost time.”

For example, let’s take a 60-year-old who earns £70,000, has a pension pot worth £300,000 and is in a company pension scheme which pays a total contribution of 10pc (combining employee contribution, employer contribution and tax relief).

If they keep making the same contributions until age 67, they could have a pension worth around £456,000, according to calculations from AJ Bell.

This would generate £114,000 of tax-free cash and a £342,000 taxable pot. If just the taxable part is used to provide an income, they’d be looking at a starting income of around £14,800.

But, add £15,000 (inclusive of tax relief) each year for the final five years they’re in work, on top of their usual contributions, and this pot could be worth around £540,000 when they turn 67.

You’re then talking about tax-free cash of £135,000 and a taxable pot worth £405,000 which, using the same assumptions, could provide an income of around £17,600, increasing by 2pc per year, for around 30 years.

Three years before retirement

5. Request a quote for any final salary pensions

Obtaining accurate information on final salary pensions can be slow and hard to decipher – often telling you what your pension was worth when you left service years ago, but giving little information on what to expect when you need it.

And don’t assume it will be worth more if you defer taking it. “That can be a false calculation as it ignores the income you would have received in the meantime, so do the maths and don’t be shy about taking pensions early if that’s what you need to do,” said Mr Millward.

6. Pay off debt

Clearing debt should be a priority. While borrowing rates have dropped in the past couple of years, they are still much higher than the rock-bottom rates seen in 2022. There’s also potentially huge comfort in being debt-free being debt-free.

7. Build up emergency savings

Volatile markets are challenging even with the security of a job behind you, but the stress can escalate when you don’t have that security and, instead of paying in, are now drawing from your pension.

“Investment returns are notoriously unreliable, so always assume your first year of retirement will coincide with a stock market crash and plan accordingly,” said Mr Millward. “What that really means is: keep a healthy cash float.”

In retirement, you may want to have 12 to 18 months’ expenses in an emergency fund you can freely access, though there is no set figure for this – it really boils down to what you need to buy you peace of mind.

One year before retirement

8. Review your outgoings

Costs often fall significantly in retirement. Scrutinise your essential outgoings to try and work out how much you may need each month – a budgeting plan such as the 50/30/20 method, or zero-based budgeting could help.

It’s also a good time to review costs such as insurance and utility suppliers to try to reduce prices where you can.

9. Decide on pension options, including deferring your state pension

At this point you’ll be making final decisions about entering drawdown, buying an annuity or taking ad-hoc lump sums from your pension pot.

When it comes to drawdown, a good rule of thumb on how much you can draw from your pensions and maintain the real value of the capital, is generally regarded as around 4pc a year. However, you may need to adjust this figure depending on market performance and inflation, along with other costs that can eat into your pension returns.

The real differentiator here is charges, with most financial advisers charging around 2pc a year, said Mr Millward: “This is an enormous bite out of anyone’s savings.”

“If you are taking 4pc you need total returns above 6pc a year before you even start protecting the value of your money against inflation,” he added.

“With that level of charges, 3.5pc or even 3pc might be a more realistic withdrawal level. But if you can get total charges down to around 1pc, then 4pc is probably still a realistic starting assumption – and a healthy pay rise for you.”

It’s also possible to defer your state pension in return for a higher guaranteed income. Whether this is sensible will depend on several factors, including your health, income sources and spending needs.

10. Factor in the tax issues of part-time working

Deciding when and how to stop working is a deeply personal decision, and one of the biggest transitions in our lives. Many people ease their way into retirement by reducing their working hours over time and, in parallel, gradually increasing their pension withdrawals.

Be aware that there will be tax implications if you work while drawing a pension.

The first 25pc of a pension can be taken as a tax-free lump sum, while the rest is viewed as taxable income and subject to income tax.

When added to your other income in the tax year, you may be pushed into a higher tax bracket. This is more likely if you take a large amount from your pension in one go.

Once you start withdrawing money from your pension then the money purchase annual allowance (MPAA) kicks in. This limits the amount of money you can contribute to your pension to £10,000 each tax year.

data.title: The West has woken up to China’s threat far too late
data.description:
Giving businesses the freedom to do their thing while we maintain national security is much more difficult than it looks. Two stories from last week show how much harder.

For two decades now, the jewels of our industry have been vanishing or turning red. In their different ways, iRobot and ASML shed light on a crisis no one really wants to acknowledge.

Last week, iRobot filed for bankruptcy and was immediately acquired by Picea Robotics, its China-based contract manufacturer. You may know iRobot for its Roomba vacuum cleaner, launched in 2002. But that’s only a part of the iRobot story.

The company was established in 1990 by a trio of MIT alumni, including Rodney Brooks – probably the US’s best known roboticist.

iRobot was DARPA’s choice for a tactical military robot in 1998, and its defence and industrial work was its mainstay for years, even as the Roomba took off.

The company’s machines were called on in emergencies – navigating the rubble of 9/11, and the Deepwater Horizon spill almost a decade later.

This combination of industrial, consumer and research contracts seemed like a perfect fit.

In Germany, which has a less voracious financial sector, it would have been permitted to continue to excel at all three. But Wall Street didn’t like it one bit.

A hedge fund called Red Mountain Capital told iRobot chairman and co-founder Colin Angle he was spending too much on research and development (R&D).

The fund wanted the cash to go on share buybacks instead.

Red Mountain called iRobot’s VC investments an “egregious and abusive use of shareholder capital”.

Therefore, iRobot reluctantly sold off its defence and industrial businesses to a private equity firm in 2016.

Now it had to go head to head with better capitalised rivals in the brutal consumer market, where forging a distribution channel is difficult and expensive.

A sure sign that iRobot had hollowed itself out was when it assigned the design to a Chinese contract manufacturer.

One reviewer last year reported that Roombas now “looked like much of the midrange competition: a generic, lidar-based robot vac with the Roomba name and a few design tweaks plastered on”. The models tested had “significant issues”.

So the iRobot’s story was an emblematic one: the West was denuding itself of industrial capacity.

The other startling news last week was that China will be producing chips comparable to the most advanced Western microprocessors years sooner than anyone predicted.

Today, the chips with the highest performance are produced by just one Dutch equipment maker, ASML.

Trying to recreate its cutting-edge extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography has been compared to the Manhattan Project to create the first atomic bomb.

In April ASML’s chairman insisted parity would take “years and years” to achieve.

However, Reuters reported that China has now reverse engineered the techniques and is on course to achieve it by 2030.

It did so with the aid of “recently retired, Chinese-born former ASML engineers and scientists”.

I shall leave aside the argument over whether this actually matters as much as some insist.

Possibly far more consequential is that we built the wrong artificial intelligence (AI), drunk on Davos talk of productivity miracles. Your pension pot may well soon reflect this generational error.

The fact is, ASML’s EUV was perceived to matter a great deal, and, like the cutting-edge Nvidia chips, was the subject of embargoes.

Arrests have been made for allegedly smuggling Nvidia chips into China. It’s akin to treason.

But, hang on a second. Why are we prohibiting the sale of confectionery manufacturing equipment to our superpower adversary, when we just sell them the sweets directly? Because that’s exactly what’s happened.

For years the US semiconductor industry has lobbied against embargoes.

ASML fought the sanctions too, arguing China’s defence sector didn’t need the latest and greatest chips.

But this month, the Silicon Valley venture capitalist David Sacks and Trump’s AI tsar has succeeded in punching holes in the Nvidia embargo too in an astonishing turn of events.

Sales of Nvidia’s H200 chips to China will resume, and the US government will retain a 25pc cut.

Sacks’ argument is that direct sales to China will disincentivise home-grown technology. Some chance of that.

China wants something, and thanks to the greed and short-sightedness of the tech sector it will get it.

The moral of iRobot’s story is that Wall Street hates R&D and prefers financial engineering to the real thing.

The moral of the semiconductor story is that it’s Silicon Valley greed that runs foreign policy, not the state department.

Denuding the West of vital industrial capacity to appease financial interests has been going on for years.

A Pentagon report in 2021 warned that concentrated supply chains, offshoring and a “business climate that has favoured short-term shareholder earnings” had all “severely damaged” America’s ability to arm itself.

Suppliers went from two to one to none, in critical defence sectors ranging from milling to chemicals.

It’s what Karl Marx identified when he predicted, “the last capitalist we hang shall be the one who sold us the rope”.

He was saying that greedy businessmen can’t resist a deal, even if it’s a deal that ultimately kills them.

Alas, the modern, monopoly-friendly, globalist version of capitalism has left us in the same spot. A war with any adversary will be very short indeed.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The BBC will have to give up the licence fee sooner or later
data.description:
The terms for the decennial review of the BBC’s Royal Charter have been set. Unsurprisingly, the Government has chosen to avoid asking the difficult question of whether the licence fee continues to make sense. While raising other forms of revenue will be considered, the tax on those consuming live media is here to stay.

This is a missed opportunity. The licence fee is already an embarrassing anachronism. The idea that a licence is required to watch live content produced by broadcasters charging their own independent fees to consumers is a bizarre legacy of early arguments over radio broadcasting. If it has failed to keep pace with the developments in media of the last century, it has certainly failed to keep pace with those in the new millennium.

Yet the BBC is financially reliant upon this structure, and desperate to retain it. This is, perhaps, unsurprising: very few organisations are in the privileged position of being able to charge not only their own customers, but those of their direct competitors. The results, however, are strictly negative.

The BBC is simultaneously desperate to retain public approval and also to maintain the line that it produces public services which would otherwise have no home. These objectives are in clear tension: the first drives it to produce the sort of content commercial stations would already produce; the second, a sort of Reithian public education. In practice, the former objective seems to dominate, and the latter instinct to be redirected into nakedly political exercises promoting the views of the organisation’s staff.
It is difficult to argue that this activity requires the subsidies given to the BBC by the licence fee, particularly when they increasingly drag Britain into disrepute. 

President Trump’s lawsuit against the broadcaster – and the long, shameful list of incidents demonstrating bias on Gaza and transgender issues – illustrate how problems for the state broadcaster can become problems for the Foreign Office.

Given this, it would have been better to rip the sticking plaster off today: make clear to the BBC that it must prepare for a future without the licence fee, and begin to separate the state from the broadcaster. This is, after all, the long-term direction of travel. As things stand, the inevitable has been postponed, and the adjustment will be all the harder when it eventually arrives.

data.title: Labour’s planning shake-up to spark ‘granny flat’ revolution
data.description:
Labour’s planning shake-up could trigger a wave of new “granny flats” being built in back gardens across the country, a think tank has said.

Under the Government’s efforts to boost housebuilding, homeowners will find it much easier to build small properties on their land as long as they are no bigger than twice the footprint of the original house.

This is intended to encourage people to make more effective use of the homes or land that they already own.

So-called granny flats – also known as accessory dwelling units and “in-law suites” in the US – are among the types of annexes falling in this category.

These are self-contained homes on the same plot of land as a main house that can be used to house family members or generate rental income.

Britain Remade, a think tank, said loosening planning rule could see a surge of these types of homes being built.

Other parts of the world have experienced a boom in so-called backyard homes after changes to their planning policies.

Sam Dumitriu, head of policy at Britain Remade, said that permission was granted for more than 100,000 garden flats in California over the course of five years after planning reforms were introduced.

More than a third of homes built in Los Angeles were these types of properties in 2023, according to state data.

Concerns over care

Mr Dumitriu said: “One big benefit of letting people build granny flats in their garden is that granny, or grandad, can live nearby.”

He added: “At a time when social care costs grow ever larger and nearly a million elderly people experience loneliness on a regular basis, that must be a good thing.”

He also believed homeowners could be in for a potential windfall if they all club together to open up their back gardens so that a block of homes can sit on the land.

Mr Dumitriu said: “It could potentially be something where everyone ends up a few hundred grand richer, if not more.”

The policy is part of Labour’s proposed overhaul of England’s national planning policy framework, which Mr Dumitriu called the “biggest change in planning policy in 80 years”.

Its inclusion in the proposals mean that councils can still refuse applications for such building projects, but could find it much more difficult to argue their case if the homeowner decides to appeal the planning rejection.

Other major proposed policies include default approval for new homes within walking distance of train, Tube and tram stations, including on green belt land.

New homes will also have to include special bricks for endangered birds. However, environmental rules will be loosened for smaller housebuilding projects.

Councils will also essentially be barred from setting stricter standards than those set by the Government to prevent local Government officials to stalling developments.

Developers could also be granted the freedom to opt out of providing affordable housing at a development, instead handing that money to councils to spend on social housing.

Labour has said rewriting the planning rulebook to include these policies will help to hit its target to build 1.5 million homes by the end of parliament.

data.title: Culture Secretary to review Daily Mail’s bid for The Telegraph
data.description:
The Culture Secretary has said she will review a planned takeover of The Telegraph by the owner of the Daily Mail as she pursues a “timely” conclusion to a protracted ownership saga.

Lisa Nandy on Wednesday confirmed she had received a formal request from RedBird IMI, the Abu Dhabi-backed fund that was blocked from taking control of The Telegraph, to sell on its interest to Lord Rothermere’s Daily Mail and General Trust (DMGT).

Ms Nandy said she would consider the deal on public interest grounds as well as under new laws banning foreign state influence in British newspapers.

In a written statement to Parliament, she wrote: “My priority remains building a constructive path toward a timely sale, without further delay, that is in the public interest.”

Ms Nandy will now decide whether to refer the £500m takeover for scrutiny by regulators Ofcom and the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA).

Ofcom would consider the impact of the deal on public interest considerations such as plurality, free expression of opinion and accurate news, while the CMA would review any impact on competition.

Lord Rothermere has said the deal will be funded with new lending from long-term banking partner NatWest. An initial payment of £400m will be followed by the remaining £100m in two years.

A combined Telegraph and Daily Mail would represent 30pc of national news group revenues. However, DMGT is expected to argue that growing competition from tech companies such as Google and Facebook means the deal poses no concerns.

Media analysts at Enders described the deal as “good news” for the industry, adding that regulatory clearance could be granted as soon as March. However, it is possible that Ms Nandy will choose to launch a more in-depth investigation by the CMA, which would take several months.

The proposed deal will raise hopes of a resolution to an extended period of uncertainty at The Telegraph, which has been stuck in limbo since June 2023, when the newspaper was seized by Lloyds Banking Group over £1.2bn in unpaid loans owed by former owners, the Barclay family.

RedBird IMI, which is 75pc backed by Sheikh Mansour bin Zayed Al Nahyan, the United Arab Emirates vice president, stepped in to derail an auction of The Telegraph led by Lloyds by helping to pay off the Barclay family debt.

But its takeover was blocked following an outcry in Parliament over press freedom that led to new laws against foreign state ownership of newspapers.

The bidder returned with a revised offer fronted by RedBird Capital, the US private equity fund that had been the junior partner in the initial approach. Gerry Cardinale, the RedBird Capital boss, assembled a consortium that included Lord Rothermere and Sir Leonard Blavatnik, the billionaire industrialist, as minority shareholders. The UAE stake was watered down to 15pc.

However, Mr Cardinale appeared to struggle to raise the full £500m and ultimately abandoned his bid amid ongoing concerns about foreign state influence caused by the UAE stake and RedBird’s links to China.

DMGT has insisted that its bid is free from foreign state interference and that The Telegraph will remain editorially independent.

In a statement earlier this week, the publisher said: “DMGT is determined to proceed at pace towards a resolution that brings much-needed certainty to Telegraph Media Group employees after a protracted period of instability.

“DMGT’s track record and long-standing commitment to quality, trusted journalism will bolster the UK’s economic growth agenda and ensure that The Telegraph remains a beacon of authoritative news and commentary amid competition from myriad digital and social media news sources, many of them unreliable, and powerful global online platforms.”

data.title: BP is drinking in the last-chance saloon
data.description:
From net zero to potential hero to plain old zero.

Welcome to another day at the BP circus, where its chief executive has gone from the man who helped engineer its green transformation to the person tasked with reversing it, to being fired through the roof of the big top covered in custard pie in lightning-quick fashion.

You’ve got to feel for Murray Auchincloss. It was obvious from the get-go to pretty much everyone – except somehow the desperately insular folk at BP – that he was the wrong man to drag the company out of the woke hole that it had dug itself into.

After all, he was part of the old regime that had insisted a whole-hearted embrace of all things green was the right strategy for an organisation that had spent the last 100 years furiously extracting oil and gas from every corner of the globe.

So having helped march the BP faithful reluctantly to the top of the renewables hill, the idea that Auchincloss was the man to march them back down again five minutes later was preposterous thinking. It smacked of an organisation that had completely run out of ideas.

Still, the manner of his exit is no less chaotic – everything about it is absurd.

The Canadian is out with immediate effect, less than two years into the job. His replacement – American executive Meg O’Neill, who currently runs Australian oil and gas firm Woodside Energy – won’t take the reins until April next year.

Until then Carol Howle, BP’s head of supply, trading and shipping, will run the show. As for Auchincloss, he gets to hang around in “an advisory role” for another year.

Albert Manifold, the new chairman, deserves credit for acting decisively – particularly since he’s only been in the role officially since the beginning of October. Yet why BP felt the need to rush out an announcement at 10pm is a mystery.

Perhaps the thinking was that shareholders would need to stay up all night just to get their heads around yet another convoluted mess that the board has contrived to create.

Amid this utter shambles, O’Neill has to be the one that finally gets things right – because through nothing other than acute self-harm, BP is now well and truly drinking in the last chance saloon.

Get this wrong and BP will surely find itself on the receiving end of a takeover bid from a bigger, better-run competitor.

It has been reported that Greg Gut, Shell’s chief dealmaker, quit the oil and gas giant in September after Wael Sawan, the chief executive, and other top bosses blocked an internal proposal to buy its fallen arch-rival. Surely though, if BP’s troubles persist, there comes a point at which a bid becomes impossible to resist?

A devastating break-up of BP can’t be ruled out either. According to reports, United Arab Emirates’s state-owned oil company ADNOC is keen to get its hands on BP’s gas fields.

With Auchincloss’s hapless predecessor, Bernard Looney, now a senior ADNOC executive, it is hard to think of a more humiliating end for one of Britain’s most illustrious institutions.

It’s hard to know what would represent the more ignominious conclusion: dismemberment at the hands of others or shareholders giving up altogether and instructing a company that has nowhere left to go to run itself down and hand the cash back to investors. Neither can be ruled out at this stage.

O’Neill is joining an organisation whose turmoil has become truly existential.

Her priority will be to speed up BP’s retreat from renewables – which won’t be too taxing, given that it was in danger of looking positively pedestrian and half-hearted under Auchincloss.

Ashley Kelty, analyst at Panmure Gordon, said his “uninspired leadership has seen BP underperform peers for a number of years”.

“We would imagine that investors will be thrilled to see the end of the woke management ethos that has prevailed under Auchincloss. We hope that BP will now return to focusing on its core oil and gas business and could see a return to growth,” he added.

Biraj Borkhataria, an RBC analyst, described Auchincloss’s departure as a case of “when, not if”. 

“Murray has tried to right the ship following the strategic errors in 2020-21, however it is clear that the business has been impaired by divesting the wrong assets at the wrong time (oil at the bottom of the cycle) and buying the wrong ones at the wrong time (renewables at the top),” he said.

Even Auchincloss had little faith in himself, it seems. When Manifold became chair, Auchincloss expressed his “openness” to stepping down if BP could find someone to “accelerate delivery of BP’s strategy”, he said. 

One imagines the board began their search for a successor right away after that bombshell.

The other big task for O’Neill will be to put the rocket-boosters under debt reduction – but to do that, she must first confront the true scale of BP’s financial strain.

The company’s reported net debt figure is $26bn (£19.4bn) but analysts point out that it ignores a series of large liabilities lurking on its books. BP’s debt load jumps to nearly $64bn if you include $17.1bn of hybrid bonds, $13.6bn of lease obligations and $7bn of outstanding liabilities relating to the catastrophic Gulf of Mexico oil spill.

O’Neill needs to consider whether BP’s reporting is up to scratch. Yet even then, BP’s fate is not entirely in its own hands. 

Not only is it at the mercy of an oil price that is going in the wrong direction but it has made debt reduction contingent on the sale of several assets that some in the City think BP has overvalued. Kelty recently described a pledge to slash BP’s net debt to between $14bn and $18bn by 2027 as “fanciful”.

Borkhataria thinks the oil giant needs to abandon its share buyback programme entirely so that it can “repair the balance sheet further”. However, BP bosses will be loath to risk the wrath of investors whose shares are worth a third of what they were at the turn of the century.

BP says that O’Neill’s “proven track record of driving transformation, growth and disciplined capital allocation makes her the right leader”. 

No doubt her dismissal of “zealous” young people, for taking an ideological stance against fossil fuels, added to her appeal.

However, Borkhataria points out that Woodside’s share price has “materially” lagged peers over the last five years – including BP.

It would be unfair to write off the American before she’s even through the door – but only BP could decide the answer to its crisis lies at one of the few companies whose performance has been worse than its own.

data.title: Renationalised trains to be red, white and blue
data.description:
Renationalised trains will be given a Union flag-inspired red, white and blue livery, the Government has announced.

State-owned company Great British Railways (GBR) is to unveil its branding on Tuesday, before it is rolled out to trains, websites and stations from next spring.

Stephen Bayley, founder of the Design Museum, criticised the new colour scheme.

Mr Bayley said: “It’s atrocious. A mad dog’s breakfast. A livery is branding and branding is all about associations and expectations.

“In this sense, they’ve got it right. It projects the values of the sponsoring organisation: artless, careless, clumsy, unintelligent and uncoordinated.”

Referring to Japan’s bullet trains, he continued: “Have they never seen a Japanese Shinkansen? That’s what a train should look like.

“We have some of the best designers in the world in this country. A pity they were not hired by Great British Railways.”

However, the Department for Transport said the “striking and memorable design” was developed in-house, to maximise value for money.

The new livery will mark a stark change from the “Rail Blue” colour scheme that defined publicly owned British Rail’s trains from the mid-1960s to the 1980s.

Christian Wolmar, a rail historian and author, said he found the new GBR livery “impressive”, saying: “I think it conveys an impression of speed. It’s elegant. It combines Union Jack colours, and I think it’s very important to have an identity.

“The design is bloody fabulous. I just wonder if it’s a bit too modern, whether they could have reflected at all on the past, but I have to commend it.”

The seven major train operators that have already nationalised have kept the branding of their previous operators.

The Government has said that existing train companies will rebrand as Great British Railways, a process that has now been brought forward to next year from the expected date of 2027.

Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, said: “I’m immensely proud to unveil the new look for Great British Railways as we deliver landmark legislation to nationalise our trains and reform the railway so it better serves passengers.

“This isn’t just a paint job, it represents a new railway, casting off the frustrations of the past and focused entirely on delivering a proper public service for passengers.

“With fares frozen, a bold new look and fundamental reforms becoming law, we are building a railway Britain can rely on and be proud of.”
